questions.stem: A researcher studies protein digestion by measuring proteolytic activity across a range of pH values. One enzyme retains full activity at a pH of approximately 2.0. Which of the following enzymes is this most likely to be?

explanation.summary: Pepsin is the gastric protease that is activated by, and functions within, the acidic environment of the stomach.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pepsin is secreted as the zymogen pepsinogen and is activated by the acidic stomach environment, where it hydrolyzes peptide bonds with very broad specificity. Its activity at low pH is essential to begin digesting denatured dietary proteins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carboxypeptidase is a pancreatic enzyme secreted into the small intestine, where bicarbonate neutralizes the chyme; it does not function at pH 2.0.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glutamate dehydrogenase is an intracellular nitrogen-handling enzyme of tissues such as liver and muscle, not an acid-active gastric protease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alanine transaminase acts within bodily tissues at near-neutral pH and does not operate in the acidic stomach.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Trypsin is a pancreatic protease that works in the small intestine after bicarbonate neutralizes the acidic chyme, not at pH 2.0.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: During catabolism of an amino acid, the nitrogen is removed from the carbon skeleton and can be redirected into biosynthesis. Which of the following molecules can be synthesized using this liberated nitrogen?

explanation.summary: Nitrogen freed from amino acids is used to build nitrogen-containing biomolecules such as the purine and pyrimidine bases.
explanation.whyCorrect: One of the three defined fates of amino acid components is the biosynthesis of nitrogen-containing compounds, including nitrogenous bases for DNA and RNA. Adenine is a nitrogen-rich purine base whose synthesis requires nitrogen donors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-ketoglutarate is the carbon skeleton left behind after nitrogen is removed from glutamate; the molecule itself contains no nitrogen.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose can be made from the carbon skeleton of certain amino acids, but glucose contains no nitrogen and is not built from the liberated amino group.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbon dioxide arises from oxidative catabolism of the carbon backbone, not from the amino-group nitrogen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fumarate is a carbon-based TCA cycle intermediate that lacks nitrogen and does not incorporate the liberated amino group.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A 2-day-old male becomes progressively lethargic after feeding and begins to breathe rapidly. Within an hour he is nearly comatose, responding only to painful stimuli. Laboratory studies show marked hyperammonemia, and a urea cycle disorder is suspected. Which amino acid is most likely to be elevated in this patient before treatment?

explanation.summary: When ammonia is in excess, glutamine synthetase drives accumulation of glutamine, the osmotically active nitrogen carrier.
explanation.whyCorrect: Elevated ammonia stimulates glutamine synthetase, which consumes glutamate to package nitrogen into glutamine. Because glutamine is the predominant nitrogen transporter and accumulates when the liver cannot dispose of ammonia, it rises in hyperammonemic states.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glutamate is typically depleted, not elevated, because it is rapidly consumed by glutamine synthetase as ammonia rises.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glycine is not a primary participant in the ammonia-buffering shuttle and would not be expected to rise from hyperammonemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Histidine can be deaminated as a source of ammonia but is not the amino acid that accumulates in response to excess ammonia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Asparagine can undergo deamination but does not accumulate as a nitrogen buffer during hyperammonemia.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A biochemist characterizes an enzyme that transfers the amino group of one amino acid onto an alpha-keto acid, effectively placing nitrogen on a new carbon skeleton without releasing free ammonia. This class of enzyme requires which of the following cofactors?

explanation.summary: Transaminases (aminotransferases) require pyridoxal phosphate, a vitamin B6 derivative, to swap amino and carbonyl groups.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nearly all aminotransferases use PLP, a vitamin B6 derivative, to move the amino group of an amino acid onto an alpha-keto acid. This allows nitrogen to flow safely between carbon carriers without being released as toxic ammonia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Biotin is the cofactor for carboxylase reactions, not for amino-group transfer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thiamine pyrophosphate serves dehydrogenase and transketolase reactions, not transamination.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cobalamin (B12) is used by methylmalonyl-CoA mutase and methionine synthase, not by transaminases.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tetrahydrofolate carries one-carbon units and is not required for transamination.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: In peripheral tissues, a carbon skeleton that has already accepted one nitrogen as glutamate accepts a second nitrogen in an ATP-dependent, irreversible reaction to form the nitrogen-rich blood carrier. Which enzyme catalyzes this step?

explanation.summary: Glutamine synthetase irreversibly adds a second ammonia onto glutamate, using ATP, to form glutamine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glutamate can accept another nitrogen through the action of glutamine synthetase, producing the nitrogen-rich carrier glutamine. The reaction consumes ATP and is irreversible, so a separate enzyme is required to reverse it in the liver.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glutaminase performs the opposite reaction, hydrolyzing glutamine back to glutamate and ammonia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glutamate dehydrogenase forms glutamate from alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia; it does not make glutamine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspartate transaminase swaps amino and keto groups between amino acids and does not incorporate free ammonia into glutamine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I incorporates ammonia into carbamoyl phosphate for the urea cycle, not onto glutamate to form glutamine.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A patient develops metabolic acidosis, and the kidney responds by generating ammonia to buffer the urine. This process begins with an enzyme that hydrolyzes glutamine to glutamate while releasing an ammonium ion. Which enzyme catalyzes this reaction?

explanation.summary: Glutaminase uses water to cleave ammonia from glutamine, supplying ammonia the kidney uses to resolve acidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Glutaminase hydrolyzes glutamine to glutamate and an ammonium ion. In the kidney it acts as an ammonia depot, using its ready supply of glutamine to liberate ammonia that buffers the urine and helps rapidly resolve metabolic acidosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glutamine synthetase makes glutamine by adding ammonia to glutamate, the reverse of what is needed here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glutamate dehydrogenase acts on glutamate (not glutamine) to release the second ammonia; the first ammonia comes from glutaminase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alanine transaminase swaps functional groups without releasing free ammonia from glutamine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pepsin is a gastric protease and has no role in renal ammonia generation.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A physiology lecture emphasizes how the body moves nitrogen safely between tissues to avoid releasing toxic ammonia. Which molecule serves as the predominant nitrogen transporter in the blood?

explanation.summary: Glutamine, which carries two nitrogens per molecule, is the predominant nitrogen transporter in the blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because glutamine holds two nitrogens per amino acid, it is nitrogen-rich and serves as the predominant ammonia transporter in the blood, shuttling nitrogen from peripheral tissues to the liver for urea production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alanine can carry nitrogen, but with only one amino group it is not the predominant blood carrier described here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glutamate is the substrate that is loaded and unloaded within cells rather than the main circulating nitrogen carrier.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Urea is the excretory waste form of nitrogen produced in the liver, not the amino acid transporter that delivers nitrogen to it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aspartate donates a nitrogen within the urea cycle but is not the predominant nitrogen carrier in the blood.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with a history of liver cirrhosis is brought to the emergency department by his daughter because he is confused and sleepy. He is disoriented to time, and when his wrists are extended his hands make a jerky, flapping movement. Which of the following laboratory tests will be most helpful in establishing the diagnosis?

explanation.summary: In suspected hepatic encephalopathy, a serum ammonia level confirms hyperammonemia from impaired hepatic urea production.
explanation.whyCorrect: The flapping tremor (asterixis), confusion, and lethargy in a cirrhotic patient point to hepatic encephalopathy. A greatly elevated serum ammonia reflects the failing liver's reduced ability to convert ammonia to urea and supports the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Serum glucose does not identify the ammonia-driven mechanism responsible for this presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Serum lipase is used to evaluate pancreatitis, not hepatic encephalopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum creatinine assesses renal function and does not confirm hyperammonemic encephalopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serum albumin reflects hepatic synthetic capacity but does not directly demonstrate the elevated ammonia causing his symptoms.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A patient with severe hyperammonemia develops cerebral edema and rising intracranial pressure. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of the astrocyte swelling in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of glutamine as an osmolyte draws water into astrocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ammonia binds directly to neuronal membranes, rupturing them

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperglycemia increases plasma osmolarity and dehydrates neurons

choices.label: D
choices.text: Iron deposition within astrocytes triggers oxidative lysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lactate crossing the blood-brain barrier directly lyses astrocytes

explanation.summary: Excess ammonia drives glutamine synthesis, and glutamine acts as an osmolyte that pulls water into astrocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: As ammonia rises, glutamine synthetase produces ever-increasing amounts of glutamine. Glutamine is an active osmolyte, so its accumulation promotes water influx and osmotic swelling of astrocytes, leading to cerebral edema and increased intracranial pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The injury results from osmotic swelling, not from ammonia directly rupturing neuronal membranes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyperglycemia is not the described mechanism and would tend to draw water out of cells, not cause swelling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron deposition is not part of the ammonia neurotoxicity mechanism taught here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Although hyperammonemia can be associated with lactic acidosis, lactate is not the cause of the astrocyte osmotic swelling.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: In peripheral tissues, free ammonia is captured by adding it to a TCA cycle intermediate to form glutamate, and the same enzyme reverses the reaction in the liver to liberate ammonia for urea synthesis. Which enzyme catalyzes this reversible reaction?

explanation.summary: Glutamate dehydrogenase reversibly incorporates ammonia into alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate, using NAD(P).
explanation.whyCorrect: Glutamate dehydrogenase reversibly joins free ammonia to the TCA intermediate alpha-ketoglutarate to make glutamate, using NAD(P)+/NAD(P)H. It loads nitrogen in peripheral tissues and runs in reverse in the liver to release ammonia for urea production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glutamine synthetase adds ammonia onto glutamate to form glutamine using ATP; it does not act on alpha-ketoglutarate.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Glutaminase removes ammonia from glutamine, not from alpha-ketoglutarate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pepsin is a gastric protease with no role in ammonia incorporation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carboxypeptidase is a pancreatic protease and does not handle nitrogen incorporation.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A digestive enzyme is secreted in an inactive form and only functions after bicarbonate neutralizes the acidic chyme entering the small intestine. Which of the following enzymes best fits this description?

explanation.summary: Trypsin is a pancreatic protease secreted into the small intestine, where bicarbonate neutralizes the acidic chyme.
explanation.whyCorrect: Trypsin, along with chymotrypsin and carboxypeptidase, is a pancreatic enzyme secreted into the small intestine together with bicarbonate that neutralizes the acidic chyme. These enzymes collectively cleave peptides from a variety of proteins.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pepsin works in the acidic stomach and does not depend on bicarbonate neutralization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glutaminase is an intracellular nitrogen-handling enzyme, not a secreted digestive protease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glutamate dehydrogenase is an intracellular enzyme of nitrogen metabolism, not a luminal digestive enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aminopeptidases are produced by brush border cells rather than secreted by the pancreas with bicarbonate.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A patient with hyperammonemia is hyperventilating despite normal oxygenation and no metabolic acidosis. An arterial blood gas shows a low pCO2 and an elevated pH. Which of the following best explains this acid-base disturbance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis from ammonia effects on brainstem respiratory centers

explanation.summary: Ammonia compromises the brainstem respiratory centers, driving hyperventilation that produces respiratory alkalosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ammonia toxicity compromises the brainstem respiratory centers and causes hyperventilation in the absence of hypoxia or metabolic acidosis. Excessive exhalation of CO2 lowers arterial pCO2, raising blood pH and producing respiratory alkalosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Metabolic acidosis would lower the pH, whereas this patient has an elevated pH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Respiratory acidosis is caused by hypoventilation with a high pCO2, the opposite of the low pCO2 seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Metabolic alkalosis from vomiting is not the ammonia-driven respiratory mechanism described, and the low pCO2 reflects a respiratory process.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The abnormal low pCO2 and elevated pH indicate a true acid-base disturbance, not a normal state.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A full-term male newborn is well until 24 hours of life, when he becomes irritable and feeds poorly, then develops lethargy, vomiting, and tachypnea. Arterial blood gas shows a respiratory alkalosis. Serum ammonia is 2,800 micromol/L and serum glutamine is elevated. Urinalysis shows orotic aciduria, and a complete blood count is normal. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: OTC deficiency is the most common urea cycle defect and uniquely produces orotic aciduria with hyperammonemia but no anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is the most common urea cycle disorder. Without OTC, carbamoyl phosphate accumulates, leaks into the cytosol, and is diverted into the pyrimidine synthesis pathway to form orotic acid. This yields the combination of hyperammonemia, elevated glutamine, low/absent citrulline, and orotic aciduria with a normal CBC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPS-I deficiency causes low citrulline and hyperammonemia but produces normal or low orotic acid, because carbamoyl phosphate is not made.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Argininosuccinate synthetase deficiency (citrullinemia) causes elevated, not low, citrulline and does not cause orotic aciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Arginase deficiency presents later with spasticity and markedly elevated arginine, not neonatal orotic aciduria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: UMP synthase deficiency (hereditary orotic aciduria) causes orotic aciduria with megaloblastic anemia and NO hyperammonemia.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A 3-day-old infant with lethargy and vomiting is found to have marked hyperammonemia and orotic aciduria. Genetic and metabolic workup is pending. Which additional finding would best distinguish ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency from hereditary orotic aciduria (UMP synthase deficiency)?

explanation.summary: OTC deficiency causes hyperammonemia without anemia, whereas hereditary orotic aciduria causes anemia without hyperammonemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both disorders present with high orotic acid, so they must be differentiated. UMP synthase deficiency causes classic orotic aciduria with megaloblastic anemia but does NOT cause hyperammonemia. In contrast, OTC deficiency does present with hyperammonemia but does not cause megaloblastic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Megaloblastic anemia points toward hereditary orotic aciduria, not OTC deficiency, so it would not identify OTC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both conditions share elevated orotic acid, so this cannot distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Low BUN reflects impaired urea synthesis but is not specific and would not separate these two possibilities cleanly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Elevated glutamine accompanies hyperammonemia of urea cycle defects but is not the defining discriminator between these two.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A neonate presents with hyperammonemia and elevated serum glutamine. Plasma amino acid analysis reveals markedly elevated citrulline with low argininosuccinic acid. Which enzyme is most likely deficient?

explanation.summary: Argininosuccinate synthetase deficiency (citrullinemia) causes citrulline buildup with low argininosuccinic acid.
explanation.whyCorrect: Argininosuccinate synthetase converts citrulline into argininosuccinate. When it is deficient, its substrate citrulline accumulates while its product argininosuccinic acid (ASA) remains low. This pattern of high citrulline with low ASA defines citrullinemia and distinguishes it from lyase deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.A: OTC deficiency causes low or absent citrulline, not elevated citrulline.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Argininosuccinate lyase deficiency elevates BOTH citrulline and argininosuccinic acid, not low ASA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CPS-I deficiency causes low citrulline because upstream carbamoyl phosphate is not produced.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Arginase deficiency elevates arginine and presents with spasticity, not a citrulline/ASA imbalance.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A hyperammonemic infant has plasma studies showing elevated citrulline and elevated argininosuccinic acid. Which enzyme deficiency best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Argininosuccinate lyase deficiency (argininosuccinic aciduria) elevates both citrulline and argininosuccinic acid.
explanation.whyCorrect: Argininosuccinate lyase cleaves argininosuccinate into arginine and fumarate. When it is deficient, argininosuccinic acid accumulates, and citrulline upstream also rises. The presence of high ASA together with high citrulline defines argininosuccinic aciduria and separates it from citrullinemia, where ASA is low.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Synthetase deficiency (citrullinemia) elevates citrulline but keeps argininosuccinic acid LOW.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OTC deficiency causes low citrulline and high orotic acid, not elevated ASA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Arginase deficiency elevates arginine, not citrulline and ASA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NAGS deficiency causes low urea cycle intermediates from impaired CPS-I activation, not ASA accumulation.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child has progressive spasticity and stiff, scissoring gait present since infancy, and was initially thought to have cerebral palsy. Serum ammonia is only mildly elevated, but plasma arginine is nearly four-fold above the upper reference limit. Which enzyme is most likely deficient?

explanation.summary: Arginase deficiency uniquely presents with progressive spasticity mimicking cerebral palsy and markedly elevated arginine with only mild hyperammonemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Arginase cleaves arginine into ornithine and urea. Its deficiency causes arginine to rise three- to four-fold above normal, and the elevated arginine and arginine-derived compounds drive the neurological picture. Symptoms progress from birth with mean onset around 2 years and are often mistaken for cerebral palsy, while ammonia is only mildly high.

whyWrongByChoice.B: OTC deficiency typically presents in the neonatal period with severe hyperammonemia and orotic aciduria, not late spasticity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CPS-I deficiency causes severe neonatal hyperammonemia with low intermediates, not elevated arginine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Synthetase deficiency causes citrullinemia with high citrulline, not high arginine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lyase deficiency elevates citrulline and argininosuccinic acid, not isolated arginine with spasticity.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: An infant with recurrent hyperammonemia has a complete but poorly stimulated urea cycle. Enzymatic studies show that carbamoyl phosphate synthetase-I fails to be activated normally. Administration of carbamylglutamate corrects the hyperammonemia. Which of the following best explains why this therapy works in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It supplies the missing carbamoyl phosphate directly

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a synthetic N-acetylglutamate analog that directly activates CPS-I

choices.label: C
choices.text: It inhibits pyrimidine synthesis to lower orotic acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: It scavenges glutamine for urinary excretion

explanation.summary: In NAGS deficiency, carbamylglutamate is a synthetic NAG analog that directly activates CPS-I, bypassing the need for endogenous NAG.
explanation.whyCorrect: CPS-I is allosterically activated by N-acetylglutamate (NAG), which is made by NAG synthetase (NAGS). In NAGS deficiency the urea cycle enzymes are intact but cannot be stimulated. Carbamylglutamate is a synthetic analog of NAG that activates CPS-I directly, restoring flux; this is well tolerated and often eliminates the need for protein restriction. CPS-I deficiency itself, by contrast, is non-responsive to carbamylglutamate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carbamylglutamate is an activator of CPS-I, not a source of carbamoyl phosphate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Its benefit is activation of the urea cycle, not inhibition of pyrimidine synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glutamine scavenging is the action of phenylbutyrate, not carbamylglutamate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: It does not substitute for arginase; it acts at the CPS-I regulatory step.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A researcher studies the first committed step of the urea cycle in isolated hepatocyte mitochondria. Which of the following molecules is the physiologic allosteric activator required for carbamoyl phosphate synthetase-I activity?

explanation.summary: CPS-I is allosterically activated by N-acetylglutamate (NAG).
explanation.whyCorrect: Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase-I converts ammonia and CO2 into carbamoyl phosphate and requires allosteric activation by N-acetylglutamate. Arginine, a urea cycle intermediate, further stimulates NAG synthase to increase NAG when nitrogen-rich intermediates accumulate, ensuring augmented nitrogen disposal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspartate is a substrate that donates the second nitrogen at the argininosuccinate synthetase step, not a CPS-I activator.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ornithine combines with carbamoyl phosphate at the OTC step; it does not allosterically activate CPS-I.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fumarate is a product of the lyase step that links to the TCA cycle, not a regulator of CPS-I.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Argininosuccinate is a downstream intermediate, not the CPS-I activator.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: In the urea cycle, one nitrogen of urea is derived from free ammonia, and the second nitrogen is supplied by another amino acid. This second nitrogen donor is regenerated from fumarate by way of malate and oxaloacetate. Which molecule provides this second nitrogen atom to the urea cycle?

explanation.summary: Aspartate supplies the second nitrogen of urea and is regenerated from fumarate via malate, oxaloacetate, and AST.
explanation.whyCorrect: Argininosuccinate synthetase uses aspartate to add the second nitrogen atom. The fumarate released by argininosuccinate lyase is converted to malate and then oxaloacetate, which is transaminated by aspartate aminotransferase (AST) using glutamate as the nitrogen donor to regenerate aspartate, sustaining nitrogen flow into urea.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glutamine is a major nitrogen transporter to the liver but is not the direct second-nitrogen donor within the cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ornithine is the carrier that accepts carbamoyl phosphate and is regenerated by arginase; it does not donate the second nitrogen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alanine transports nitrogen to the liver in the Cahill cycle but does not directly provide the second urea nitrogen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Citrulline is an intermediate that accepts aspartate; it is not itself the second nitrogen donor.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A child with a urea cycle defect and chronic hyperammonemia is treated with sodium phenylbutyrate. Which of the following best describes why removing this drug's target molecule helps control the patient's ammonia levels?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It binds glutamine for urinary excretion

choices.label: B
choices.text: It binds urea directly to accelerate its transport to the kidney

choices.label: C
choices.text: It inhibits arginase to prevent ammonia release

choices.label: D
choices.text: It neutralizes ammonia by acidifying the blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: It replaces the deficient urea cycle enzyme

explanation.summary: Phenylbutyrate (via phenylacetate) conjugates glutamine, the predominant nitrogen transporter, providing a urea-independent route to excrete nitrogen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylbutyrate is oxidized to phenylacetate, which covalently binds glutamine to form a derivative excreted in the urine. Because glutamine is the predominant nitrogen transporter in the body, its removal provides an alternative path for nitrogen disposal that bypasses the compromised urea cycle, keeping ammonia within a safer range.

whyWrongByChoice.B: It does not bind urea; it conjugates glutamine, and the whole point is to bypass urea production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: It works by scavenging glutamine, not by inhibiting arginase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: It does not lower ammonia through blood acidification; it removes nitrogen as a glutamine conjugate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitrogen scavengers do not replace the deficient enzyme; they provide an alternate excretion route.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: During prolonged vigorous exercise, skeletal muscle transfers amino acid nitrogen to a carrier that travels to the liver, where it is converted to pyruvate for gluconeogenesis and its nitrogen enters the urea cycle. Which molecule serves as this nitrogen carrier?

explanation.summary: In the glucose-alanine (Cahill) cycle, alanine carries nitrogen from muscle to the liver and its carbon skeleton becomes pyruvate for gluconeogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alanine is a key nitrogen transporter, especially during prolonged intense exercise. Via ALT, muscle nitrogen is loaded onto pyruvate to form alanine, which travels to the liver; there ALT converts alanine back to pyruvate for gluconeogenesis, and the released nitrogen feeds urea synthesis. This process is known as the Cahill cycle.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lactate carries carbon in the Cori cycle but does not transport nitrogen, and no transaminase is involved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Creatinine is a waste product of creatine metabolism, not a gluconeogenic nitrogen shuttle.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Urea is the excreted nitrogen end product, not the muscle-to-liver carrier.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxaloacetate is an intermediate that regenerates aspartate; it is not the muscle nitrogen carrier.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A physiologist compares two interorgan shuttles. In one, muscle and erythrocytes send a three-carbon molecule to the liver to be remade into glucose without any amino acid metabolism. Which statement best characterizes this Cori cycle relative to the glucose-alanine cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It transports lactate and requires no transaminase enzymes

choices.label: B
choices.text: It transports alanine and requires ALT

choices.label: C
choices.text: It generates urea directly in skeletal muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: It occurs only in the presence of insulin

choices.label: E
choices.text: It converts ammonia to glutamine in erythrocytes

explanation.summary: The Cori cycle recycles lactate from muscle and erythrocytes back to glucose in the liver and, unlike the Cahill cycle, needs no transaminases.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Cori cycle allows lactate produced by anaerobic glycolysis in muscle or by erythrocytes to be returned to the liver and remade into glucose. Because it does not involve amino acid metabolism, no amino transaminases are required, in contrast to the glucose-alanine cycle which relies on ALT.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Transporting alanine and requiring ALT describes the glucose-alanine (Cahill) cycle, not the Cori cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Skeletal muscle does not run the urea cycle; urea is made in the liver.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The lactate-recycling Cori cycle is not defined by insulin presence; the glucose-alanine cycle notably does not occur when insulin is present.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Erythrocytes releasing lactate is central to the Cori cycle; it does not convert ammonia to glutamine here.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A previously healthy adult undergoes a prolonged fast during which glucagon promotes breakdown of body protein for energy. Compared with a fed state, which of the following best describes this person's nitrogen balance and its cause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Negative nitrogen balance because protein catabolism causes nitrogen loss to exceed intake

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive nitrogen balance because tissue is being actively built

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nitrogen equilibrium because intake equals loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive nitrogen balance because the urea cycle is downregulated

choices.label: E
choices.text: Negative nitrogen balance because urea synthesis is suppressed by glucagon

explanation.summary: Fasting drives a catabolic, negative nitrogen balance because nitrogen loss exceeds intake as body protein is broken down.
explanation.whyCorrect: Negative nitrogen balance occurs when nitrogen loss exceeds intake, indicating a catabolic state in which the body breaks down its own proteins. During fasting, glucagon promotes protein catabolism for energy from the amino acid carbon skeletons; this generates ammonia that is converted to urea and upregulates the urea cycle, pushing the individual toward negative nitrogen balance.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Positive nitrogen balance is an anabolic state seen in growth, pregnancy, and recovery, not fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Equilibrium is typical of healthy adults on a balanced diet, not during a prolonged fast.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Starvation actually upregulates the urea cycle to increase urea excretion, and the balance is negative, not positive.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucagon during fasting increases, not suppresses, urea synthesis; the balance is negative because of increased catabolism.

questions.topicName: Amino Acid Metabolism I: Nitrogen Balance and Urea Cycle

questions.stem: A 2-day-old term female is feeding well but her urine has a distinct sweet, burnt-sugar odor noted by nursing staff. The newborn screen shows markedly elevated leucine, isoleucine, and valine. The parents are given a specialized formula lacking branched-chain amino acids. A deficiency in which of the following enzymes is the most likely cause of this presentation?

explanation.summary: Maple syrup urine disease results from deficient branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase.
explanation.whyCorrect: The sweet, burnt-sugar urine odor and elevated valine, leucine, and isoleucine are classic for maple syrup urine disease (MSUD). MSUD is caused by deficiency of the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase, a TPP-dependent complex that oxidatively decarboxylates the BCAA-derived alpha-keto acids. The block causes accumulation of toxic alpha-keto acids that are excreted in the urine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes PKU, which gives a musty odor and elevated phenylalanine, not elevated BCAAs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Homogentisate oxidase deficiency causes alkaptonuria with dark urine, not sweet-smelling urine or elevated BCAAs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency causes homocystinuria with elevated homocysteine, not a maple-syrup odor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Propionyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency causes propionic acidemia; while an organic acidemia, it does not produce the maple-syrup odor or the specific BCAA elevation of MSUD.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 3-day-old newborn becomes lethargic and hypotonic before discharge. Laboratory studies show a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and organic aciduria, with no urea cycle intermediates above normal. Based on the identity of the accumulating organic acid, the physician prescribes adenosyl-cobalamin. Catabolism of which of the following is defective in this patient?

explanation.summary: Methylmalonic acidemia is a methylmalonyl-CoA mutase defect that impairs catabolism of several amino acids including threonine.
explanation.whyCorrect: The response to adenosyl-cobalamin identifies a defect in methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, the vitamin B12 (adenosyl-cobalamin)-dependent enzyme. This blocks the conversion of methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA and impairs catabolism of methionine, threonine, isoleucine, and valine, causing methylmalonic acidemia with high anion gap acidosis and hyperammonemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phenylalanine catabolism is disrupted in PKU (phenylalanine hydroxylase), which does not respond to cobalamin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Leucine catabolism is blocked in isovaleric acidemia (isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase), which does not use adenosyl-cobalamin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homocysteine metabolism is affected in homocystinuria, treated with B6 or B12 for methionine synthase, not adenosyl-cobalamin for a mutase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tyrosine catabolism is blocked in alkaptonuria, which causes dark urine and arthritis, not this acute acidosis.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy has developmental delay and poor school performance. On examination he is tall and thin with disproportionately long limbs and fingers. Laboratory studies reveal elevated plasma homocysteine, and urinalysis is unremarkable for organic acids and ketones. Genetic testing identifies a mutation in the CBS gene. Supplementation with which vitamin is most likely to be therapeutically beneficial?

explanation.summary: Cystathionine beta-synthase is PLP-dependent, so pyridoxine (B6) can rescue responsive homocystinuria variants.
explanation.whyCorrect: A CBS mutation causes homocystinuria. Cystathionine beta-synthase requires pyridoxal phosphate (PLP), the active form of vitamin B6, and some patients respond to high-dose B6 supplementation. The tall marfanoid habitus with long limbs, cognitive issues, and elevated homocysteine fit CBS deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thiamine (TPP) is the cofactor for the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase in MSUD, not for CBS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Biotin is the cofactor for propionyl-CoA carboxylase, not CBS.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B12 (methylcobalamin) supports methionine synthase; it is used when the homocystinuria is due to methionine synthase deficiency, not a CBS mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BH4 is the cofactor for phenylalanine hydroxylase in PKU, unrelated to CBS.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is evaluated for progressive back and hip pain. His wife notes that his urine turns dark, almost black, after sitting in the toilet bowl, and his sclerae and ear cartilage have a bluish-black discoloration. Which enzyme deficiency best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Alkaptonuria is a homogentisate oxidase deficiency causing homogentisic acid accumulation, ochronosis, and dark urine.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alkaptonuria results from deficient homogentisate oxidase in the tyrosine catabolic pathway. Homogentisic acid accumulates and spontaneously oxidizes and polymerizes into a dark pigment, causing urine that darkens on air exposure, bluish-black connective tissue pigmentation (ochronosis), and early-onset arthritis of the spine, hips, and knees.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tyrosinase deficiency causes albinism with hypopigmentation, not dark pigment deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes PKU with a musty odor, not dark urine or ochronosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase deficiency causes methylmalonic acidemia with acute acidosis in infancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency causes isovaleric acidemia with a sweaty-feet odor.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A newborn with untreated classic PKU is noted to have very fair skin, blond hair, and light-colored eyes compared with the parents. This hypopigmentation is best explained by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Absence of melanocytes in the skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deficient tyrosine available for melanin synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: A primary mutation in the tyrosinase gene

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excess homogentisic acid deposition in skin

explanation.summary: In PKU, blocked phenylalanine-to-tyrosine conversion limits tyrosine, the precursor for melanin, causing hypopigmentation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylalanine hydroxylase converts phenylalanine to tyrosine. When PAH is deficient, tyrosine becomes essential and is relatively deficient. Since tyrosine is the substrate for melanin synthesis, PKU patients characteristically show hypopigmentation of skin, hair, and eyes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PKU patients retain melanocytes; the problem is a lack of substrate, not absent pigment cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A primary tyrosinase mutation causes albinism, a separate disorder, not PKU-related hypopigmentation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homogentisic acid deposition causes the dark pigmentation of alkaptonuria, the opposite of hypopigmentation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dopamine beta-hydroxylase makes norepinephrine and is unrelated to melanin pigmentation.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 4-day-old infant presents with poor feeding, vomiting, hypotonia, and a sweaty-feet odor. Metabolic workup shows accumulation of isovaleryl-glycine and hydroxy-isovaleric acid. A deficiency in which enzyme, acting on which amino acid pathway, best explains this presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase in the leucine pathway

choices.label: B
choices.text: Propionyl-CoA carboxylase in the valine pathway

choices.label: C
choices.text: Branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase in all three BCAAs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Homogentisate oxidase in the tyrosine pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase in the methionine pathway

explanation.summary: Isovaleric acidemia is a deficiency of isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase in leucine catabolism.
explanation.whyCorrect: Isovaleric acidemia is a defect in the leucine catabolism pathway caused by deficient isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase, a mitochondrial FAD-dependent flavoenzyme that converts isovaleryl-CoA to 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA. Accumulated isovaleryl-CoA yields measurable derivatives such as isovaleryl-glycine and hydroxy-isovaleric acid, and produces the characteristic sweaty-feet odor.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Propionyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency causes propionic acidemia with propionyl-CoA buildup, not isovaleryl derivatives.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase deficiency causes MSUD affecting all three BCAAs, with a maple-syrup odor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homogentisate oxidase acts in tyrosine catabolism (alkaptonuria), unrelated to a sweaty-feet odor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase deficiency causes methylmalonic acidemia, not isovaleryl-glycine accumulation.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with severe, radiating right flank pain. Urinalysis shows hexagonal crystals, and a stone analysis confirms cystine. His disease is caused by a defect in the transporter that reabsorbs cystine along with which other group of amino acids?

explanation.summary: Cystinuria is a defect in the basic amino acid transporter that also carries the cystine dimer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cystinuria results from a defect in the basic amino acid transporter (encoded by SLC3A1 or SLC7A9), which also recognizes the disulfide-linked cysteine dimer, cystine. Affected patients cannot reabsorb ornithine, arginine, lysine, and cystine, and cystine precipitates into hexagonal kidney stones, especially in acidic urine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Branched-chain amino acids are handled by the L system and are not the defective transporter in cystinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The neutral amino acid transporter (SLC6A19) is defective in Hartnup disease, not cystinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anionic amino acids use the XAG- system, which is not involved in cystinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aromatic amino acids are transported by the L system and are not the group lost with cystine in cystinuria.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child with intermittent ataxia develops a photosensitive, pellagra-like skin rash during a period of poor diet and increased sun exposure. Urinalysis shows increased excretion of neutral amino acids including tryptophan. Which of the following best explains the pellagra-like features?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Loss of tryptophan reduces nicotinamide (niacin) production

choices.label: B
choices.text: Excess phenylalanine displaces tryptophan at the blood-brain barrier

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cystine precipitates in the renal tubules

choices.label: D
choices.text: Homogentisic acid polymerizes in connective tissue

choices.label: E
choices.text: Branched-chain alpha-keto acids inhibit the TCA cycle

explanation.summary: Hartnup disease causes neutral aminoaciduria; tryptophan loss impairs niacin synthesis and can produce pellagra.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hartnup disease is a defect in the neutral amino acid transporter (SLC6A19) causing neutral hyperaminoaciduria. Poor absorption combined with urinary loss of tryptophan reduces nicotinamide production, and when triggered by dietary changes or sun exposure can lead to pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Phenylalanine displacing tryptophan at the blood-brain barrier is a mechanism in PKU affecting serotonin, not the cause of the Hartnup skin rash.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cystine precipitation causes kidney stones in cystinuria, not a pellagra-like rash.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Homogentisic acid polymerization causes ochronosis in alkaptonuria, unrelated to pellagra.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Branched-chain alpha-keto acids inhibiting the TCA cycle relate to organic acidemias like MSUD, not Hartnup dermatitis.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: An infant is diagnosed with a severe, rare form of PKU that does not improve with strict phenylalanine restriction alone. Testing reveals a mutation in dihydropteridine reductase. Beyond a low-phenylalanine diet, why is this form more severe and require neurotransmitter precursor supplementation such as L-Dopa and 5-HTP?

choices.label: B
choices.text: BH4 cannot be recycled, impairing tyrosine and tryptophan hydroxylases

choices.label: E
choices.text: Excess methionine accumulates and is neurotoxic

explanation.summary: Malignant PKU results from failed BH4 recycling, which cripples multiple BH4-dependent hydroxylases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dihydropteridine reductase is needed to regenerate tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4). Without recycling, all BH4-dependent hydroxylases are impaired: not only phenylalanine hydroxylase but also tyrosine hydroxylase (dopamine, then norepinephrine and epinephrine) and tryptophan hydroxylase (serotonin). Because diet alone cannot restore these neurotransmitters, precursors such as L-Dopa and 5-HTP are supplemented.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Complete absence of PAH is classic PKU, which responds to phenylalanine restriction and does not by itself deplete dopamine and serotonin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tyrosinase (albinism) is not the affected enzyme in malignant PKU.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspartame is a phenylalanine source to avoid, not an enzyme inhibitor causing this severity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methionine accumulation relates to homocystinuria, not malignant PKU.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A neonate with propionic acidemia is stabilized. As part of long-term management the team plans a therapy that helps clear the accumulating toxic acid by conjugation. Which supplement is used, and by what mechanism does it help?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carnitine, which conjugates propionic acid to aid its excretion and free up CoA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Biotin, which directly degrades propionyl-CoA to acetyl-CoA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tetrahydrobiopterin, which restores propionyl-CoA carboxylase activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thiamine, which serves as the cofactor for propionyl-CoA carboxylase

choices.label: E
choices.text: Adenosyl-cobalamin, which is the cofactor for propionyl-CoA carboxylase

explanation.summary: Carnitine supplementation conjugates accumulating propionic acid to aid excretion and regenerate free CoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: In propionyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency, propionyl-CoA and propionic acid accumulate. Carnitine supplementation allows carnitine acyltransferase to conjugate carnitine to the excess acid, freeing up needed CoA and making the propionic acid more readily excreted, analogous to the handling of medium-chain acyl-CoAs in MCAD.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Biotin is the natural cofactor for propionyl-CoA carboxylase, but it does not directly degrade propionyl-CoA to acetyl-CoA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BH4 is the PAH cofactor in PKU, not relevant to propionyl-CoA carboxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thiamine (TPP) is the cofactor for the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase, not propionyl-CoA carboxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosyl-cobalamin is the cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, the next step, not propionyl-CoA carboxylase, whose cofactor is biotin.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: During the newborn period, several inborn errors that cause accumulation of organic acids present with a characteristic laboratory triad when untreated. Which of the following combinations is most typical of these organic acidemias?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal anion gap acidosis, hypoammonemia, and alkalosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and ketosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis, hypoglycemia, and hyperkalemia only

choices.label: E
choices.text: High anion gap acidosis with low ammonia and no ketosis

explanation.summary: Untreated organic acidemias classically present with high anion gap metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, and ketosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Organic acidemias accumulate toxic acids that, along with ketosis from decreased energy yield of amino acid catabolism and inhibition of TCA cycle enzymes, cause high anion gap metabolic acidosis. The organic acids also inhibit urea cycle enzymes (notably NAG synthase), producing hyperammonemia. Hypoglycemia may also occur.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Organic acidemias cause a high, not normal, anion gap, and hyperammonemia rather than hypoammonemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The primary disturbance is a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, not respiratory alkalosis; potassium is not the defining feature.
whyWrongByChoice.D: These disorders cause acidosis, not alkalosis, and tend toward hypoglycemia rather than hyperglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ammonia is typically elevated and ketosis is usually present, the opposite of this option.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A pregnant woman with poorly controlled PKU consumes a normal, phenylalanine-rich diet throughout gestation. Her fetus is at increased risk of adverse effects even though the fetus may not have inherited PKU. Which of the following congenital findings is most characteristic of this maternal condition?

explanation.summary: Maternal PKU with high phenylalanine causes fetal phenylalanine embryopathy, notably microcephaly and congenital heart defects.
explanation.whyCorrect: When a pregnant woman with PKU consumes excess phenylalanine, transplacental phenylalanine adversely affects fetal development (phenylalanine embryopathy). Characteristic features in the affected newborn include microcephaly and congenital heart defects, even if the child did not inherit PKU.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hexagonal renal calculi are the hallmark of cystinuria, not maternal PKU.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ochronosis of cartilage is seen in alkaptonuria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bluish-black sclerae reflect ochronosis in alkaptonuria, not phenylalanine embryopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A sweaty-feet odor characterizes isovaleric acidemia, not maternal PKU effects.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Amino Acid Metabolism

questions.stem: A healthy full-term newborn develops yellowing of the skin and sclerae on the third day of life. He is feeding well and is otherwise vigorous. Total bilirubin is elevated with a predominantly unconjugated (indirect) fraction, and the jaundice resolves by two weeks of age. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the elevated bilirubin?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extrahepatic obstruction of the common bile duct

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mutation in the ABCC2 (MRP2) canalicular transporter

explanation.summary: Physiologic neonatal jaundice reflects immature hepatic conjugation combined with high red-cell turnover.
explanation.whyCorrect: In newborns the liver is still maturing, so UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UDPGT) activity is transiently reduced. Combined with increased breakdown of a large, short-lived neonatal red-cell mass, this produces a benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia that resolves as the liver matures over 1-2 weeks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoimmune hepatocyte destruction is not the cause of routine physiologic neonatal jaundice, which is developmental and self-limited.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bile duct obstruction would raise the conjugated (direct) fraction and cause pale stools and dark urine, not a self-resolving indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Absent ferrochelatase impairs heme synthesis and does not describe the mechanism of physiologic neonatal jaundice.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An ABCC2/MRP2 mutation (Dubin-Johnson) causes a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, not the transient unconjugated jaundice of the newborn.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A 3-year-old who lives in a home built in the 1940s presents with irritability, abdominal pain, and loss of previously acquired developmental milestones. A complete blood count shows a microcytic anemia, and delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) is elevated. Which pair of enzymes is most directly inhibited by the responsible toxin?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Porphobilinogen deaminase and uroporphyrinogen III synthase

explanation.summary: Lead poisoning blocks ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase, causing ALA buildup and reduced heme.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lead inhibits ALA dehydratase (causing accumulation of neurotoxic ALA) and ferrochelatase (preventing insertion of iron into protoporphyrin IX). The result is decreased heme production with microcytic anemia plus the neurologic findings of ALA accumulation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ALA synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme but is not the target of lead, and biliverdin reductase acts in heme degradation, not synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: UDP-glucuronosyltransferase and heme oxygenase participate in bilirubin metabolism, not the steps blocked by lead.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Porphobilinogen deaminase and uroporphyrinogen III synthase are not the enzymes classically inhibited by lead.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The coproporphyrinogen and protoporphyrinogen oxidases are not the primary lead-sensitive steps described in the pathway.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A 2-month-old boy is brought in for failure to thrive that began shortly after birth. Laboratory studies confirm a microcytic anemia, and a Prussian blue stain of the bone marrow shows ringed sideroblasts (iron-laden mitochondria encircling the nucleus of erythroid precursors). Which of the following is the most likely cause of his condition?

explanation.summary: X-linked sideroblastic anemia results from deficient ALAS2, the erythroid-specific isoform.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALAS2 is the erythroid isoform that drives heme synthesis for hemoglobin. Its deficiency impairs heme production, so iron accumulates in the mitochondria of erythroid precursors, forming ringed sideroblasts and causing an X-linked microcytic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ALAS1 predominates in the liver and non-erythroid tissues, not the erythroid precursors affected in ringed sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Folate deficiency causes a macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia, whereas impaired heme synthesis produces microcytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increased ferrochelatase activity would enhance iron incorporation into heme, the opposite of the defect seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Excess heme oxygenase accelerates heme degradation and does not explain ringed sideroblasts or microcytic anemia.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: The committed, rate-limiting step of heme synthesis condenses glycine and succinyl-CoA to form delta-aminolevulinic acid. Which of the following cofactors is required for this reaction, such that its deficiency can produce a microcytic (sideroblastic) anemia?

explanation.summary: ALA synthase is a PLP (vitamin B6)-dependent enzyme catalyzing the first step of heme synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The first step of heme synthesis, catalyzed by ALA synthase, requires pyridoxal phosphate (the active form of vitamin B6). Because this step is essential for heme production, B6/PLP deficiency impairs heme synthesis and can cause a microcytic, sideroblastic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic megaloblastic anemia and is not the cofactor for ALA synthase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate is required for one-carbon transfer and DNA synthesis, not for the ALA synthase reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NADPH is used by biliverdin reductase in heme degradation, not for the condensation of glycine and succinyl-CoA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biotin is a cofactor for carboxylase reactions and is not involved in the first step of heme synthesis.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: Before bilirubin can be excreted into bile, it must be conjugated with glucuronic acid to become water-soluble. In which of the following cells does this conjugation reaction occur?

explanation.summary: Bilirubin is conjugated with glucuronic acid by UDP-glucuronosyltransferase inside hepatocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unconjugated bilirubin is taken up by hepatocytes, where UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UDPGT) covalently links it to glucuronic acid. This produces water-soluble conjugated bilirubin that can then be excreted into bile.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Macrophages break down red cells and generate unconjugated bilirubin, but they do not conjugate it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renal tubular cells excrete water-soluble conjugated bilirubin but do not perform the conjugation reaction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gut bacteria convert conjugated bilirubin to urobilinogen; they do not conjugate bilirubin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Erythroid precursors synthesize hemoglobin and are not sites of bilirubin conjugation.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: During degradation of heme within splenic macrophages, the porphyrin ring is cleaved to form biliverdin. Which of the following byproducts are released in this heme oxygenase-catalyzed reaction?

explanation.summary: Heme oxygenase cleaves heme to biliverdin, releasing carbon monoxide and Fe2+.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heme oxygenase opens the porphyrin ring of heme, producing biliverdin while liberating carbon monoxide and ferrous iron (Fe2+). The iron is recycled, and this reaction is the source of endogenous CO.

whyWrongByChoice.A: NADPH is a reactant for the next step (biliverdin reductase), and glucuronic acid is added later in the liver; neither is released by heme oxygenase.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ammonia and succinyl-CoA are not products of heme cleavage; succinyl-CoA is a substrate of heme synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Urobilinogen and stercobilin are downstream gut bacterial products of conjugated bilirubin, not products of heme oxygenase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycine and CO2 relate to the synthetic (ALA synthase) side of heme metabolism, not to heme degradation.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A newborn with a markedly elevated unconjugated bilirubin that is not promptly treated becomes lethargic and later develops cerebral palsy and hearing loss. Which property of unconjugated bilirubin best accounts for this neurologic injury?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is water-soluble and freely filtered by the kidney

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is lipid-soluble and can cross the blood-brain barrier

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is covalently bound to glucuronic acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is efficiently excreted into bile

choices.label: E
choices.text: It accumulates as ringed sideroblasts in the brain

explanation.summary: Lipid-soluble unconjugated bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier, causing kernicterus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Unconjugated bilirubin is hydrophobic and lipid-soluble. When it accumulates because conjugation cannot keep pace, it crosses the blood-brain barrier and deposits in the brain, producing the irreversible neurologic damage of kernicterus (cerebral palsy, hearing/vision problems).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Unconjugated bilirubin is water-insoluble and is not filtered by the kidney; only the conjugated form is renally excreted.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conjugation with glucuronic acid defines conjugated bilirubin, which does not cross the blood-brain barrier.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Only conjugated bilirubin is efficiently excreted into bile; the unconjugated form is the neurotoxic species.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ringed sideroblasts are marrow erythroid precursors in sideroblastic anemia and have nothing to do with kernicterus.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A jaundiced neonate is placed under blue-green phototherapy lamps, and the serum bilirubin falls even though hepatic conjugation is unchanged. Which of the following best explains how phototherapy lowers the bilirubin level?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It photo-isomerizes unconjugated bilirubin into a water-soluble form

choices.label: C
choices.text: It degrades bilirubin into carbon monoxide and iron

choices.label: D
choices.text: It increases albumin binding of bilirubin in the blood

choices.label: E
choices.text: It stimulates ferrochelatase to consume bilirubin

explanation.summary: Phototherapy isomerizes unconjugated bilirubin in the skin into a water-soluble form for excretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: The conjugated ring system of bilirubin absorbs blue-green light (most effective around 460-490 nm). This energy isomerizes unconjugated bilirubin in the skin capillaries into a more water-soluble isomer that can be excreted in bile and urine without requiring hepatic conjugation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phototherapy works independently of hepatic conjugation; the stem specifies conjugation is unchanged.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CO and iron are released by heme oxygenase acting on heme, not by light acting on bilirubin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increasing albumin binding would keep bilirubin in the blood rather than promote its excretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ferrochelatase inserts iron into protoporphyrin during heme synthesis and does not consume bilirubin.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A young adult with a history of mild, intermittent jaundice undergoes laparoscopy for an unrelated reason, and the surgeon notes a strikingly glossy black liver. Laboratory studies show a predominantly conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia. Which of the following defects is most likely responsible?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Defective canalicular MRP2 transporter due to an ABCC2 mutation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A slight reduction in UDP-glucuronosyltransferase activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Excessive hemolysis of red blood cells

explanation.summary: Dubin-Johnson syndrome is a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia from a defective MRP2 (ABCC2) transporter, marked by a black liver.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dubin-Johnson syndrome is an autosomal recessive defect of the ABCC2 gene encoding MRP2, which normally exports conjugated bilirubin from hepatocytes into bile. Impaired excretion raises conjugated bilirubin and produces the characteristic glossy black liver.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Complete absence of UDPGT is Crigler-Najjar type 1, which causes severe unconjugated (not conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A slight decrease in UDPGT describes Gilbert syndrome, an unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia without a black liver.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lead inhibition of ferrochelatase impairs heme synthesis and causes anemia, not a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with a black liver.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemolysis produces an unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia because it overwhelms conjugation, not a conjugated one.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man notices mild scleral icterus during a febrile illness and again after fasting for a religious observance; each episode resolves on its own. Labs show a mildly elevated, predominantly unconjugated bilirubin with normal liver enzymes and no evidence of hemolysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Gilbert syndrome causes mild, stress- or fasting-provoked unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and is benign.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gilbert syndrome is a common (3-7%), benign disorder with a slight decrease in UDPGT. Conjugation still occurs, but stressors such as infection or fasting let heme breakdown outpace conjugation, producing a transient, self-limited unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia that needs no treatment.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Crigler-Najjar type 1 has complete absence of UDPGT and presents in infancy with severe jaundice and kernicterus, not mild episodic icterus in a young adult.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dubin-Johnson causes a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with a black liver, not an unconjugated one triggered by fasting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rotor syndrome causes a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia from impaired hepatic excretion, unlike this unconjugated pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Biliary obstruction produces a conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with pale stools and dark urine, not a benign unconjugated pattern.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a gallstone lodged in her common bile duct develops jaundice, pale clay-colored stools, and dark urine. Which of the following laboratory findings best fits her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elevated unconjugated bilirubin with absent bilirubin in the urine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated conjugated bilirubin that spills over into the urine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased stercobilin production in the gut

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ringed sideroblasts in the bone marrow

explanation.summary: Post-hepatic obstruction backs up conjugated bilirubin into blood and urine, causing pale stools and dark urine.
explanation.whyCorrect: With a bile duct obstruction the liver still conjugates bilirubin, but the water-soluble conjugated form cannot reach the gut and backs up into the blood (post-hepatic jaundice). It is then abnormally excreted in urine (dark urine), while reduced bilirubin delivery to the gut produces pale stools.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The obstruction raises the conjugated fraction, and because conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble it does appear in the urine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Obstruction reduces conjugated bilirubin reaching the gut, so stercobilin falls and stools become pale, not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ringed sideroblasts reflect a heme synthesis defect in the marrow, unrelated to biliary obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Serum conjugated bilirubin rises, not falls, when it cannot be routed out through the obstructed bile duct.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A patient with acute intermittent porphyria has an attack after starting a barbiturate and drinking alcohol, both of which induce hepatic ALAS1. The clinical team wants to reduce ALAS1-driven flux through the pathway. Which of the following most directly opposes ALAS1 induction and is used to dampen such attacks?

explanation.summary: Glucose (and heme/hemin) suppresses ALAS1, so glucose loading helps dampen porphyria attacks.
explanation.whyCorrect: Low glucose indirectly stimulates ALAS1 expression, so maintaining or raising blood glucose suppresses ALAS1 and reduces flux through the heme pathway. This is why glucose loading (and hemin, which acts like heme's feedback signal) is used to blunt acute porphyria attacks.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypoxia is a stimulator of ALAS activity, which would worsen rather than dampen an attack.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Erythropoietin induces the erythroid isoform ALAS2, not the hepatic ALAS1 relevant to this scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lead inhibits downstream enzymes (ALA dehydratase, ferrochelatase) but does not suppress ALAS1 expression.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron chelation does not suppress ALAS1; if anything, intracellular iron regulates the erythroid isoform ALAS2.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (1 of 2)

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with recurrent attacks of severe, diffuse abdominal pain lasting several days, accompanied by nausea, tingling in her arms, and anxiety. The attacks tend to coincide with her menstrual cycle and a recent low-calorie diet. Her abdomen is soft with no peritoneal signs, and a urine sample left at the bedside darkens to a port-wine color on standing. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: Acute intermittent porphyria is caused by porphobilinogen deaminase deficiency and presents with neurovisceral attacks.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP) results from deficiency of porphobilinogen deaminase (PBGD), causing accumulation of the neurotoxic intermediates ALA and PBG. This produces episodic severe abdominal pain, peripheral neuropathy, and neuropsychiatric symptoms, often triggered by fasting and hormonal changes, and the urine turns port-wine colored on standing or light exposure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ferrochelatase deficiency causes erythropoietic protoporphyria, which presents with painful cutaneous photosensitivity, not neurovisceral abdominal attacks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, a cutaneous porphyria with blistering photosensitivity rather than acute abdominal pain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ALA synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme that is upregulated in attacks; its deficiency does not cause AIP and would reduce, not increase, ALA and PBG.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ALA dehydrase deficiency is a different, far rarer porphyria and is not the classic cause of the described AIP presentation.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman is admitted with an acute attack of acute intermittent porphyria marked by severe abdominal pain and confusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention to abort the attack, and what is its mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oral iron supplementation, which restores heme synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: A barbiturate sedative, which calms neurovisceral symptoms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous glucose and hemin, which downregulate ALA synthase

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-dose vitamin C, which enhances porphyrin excretion

explanation.summary: Acute porphyria attacks are treated by suppressing ALA synthase with glucose or hemin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute attacks are managed with glucose or hemin infusions, which downregulate ALA synthase (ALAS1). This reduces production of the upstream neurotoxic intermediates ALA and PBG that drive the neurovisceral symptoms. Givosiran, an siRNA against the ALAS1 transcript, works by the same principle of lowering ALA synthase activity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Iron is a substrate for the final ferrochelatase step but does not treat AIP; the defect is upstream at PBGD, and giving iron would not lower ALA and PBG.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Barbiturates induce cytochrome P450 enzymes and increase heme demand, which is a classic trigger that worsens AIP attacks.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Phlebotomy is a treatment for iron-overload states such as PCT or hemochromatosis, not for aborting an acute AIP attack.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin C aids dietary iron reduction and absorption but has no role in downregulating ALA synthase or aborting acute porphyria attacks.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 49-year-old man is referred to dermatology for painful, blistering lesions on the dorsum of both hands that appeared after a beach vacation, though he never developed a frank sunburn. Exam shows 1-2 cm blisters, some ruptured, with fragile skin over the hands and forearms. He drinks alcohol regularly, and labs reveal elevated liver enzymes. Which enzyme deficiency best explains his condition?

explanation.summary: Porphyria cutanea tarda is due to reduced uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity and causes delayed blistering photosensitivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT), the most common porphyria, results from deficiency or inhibition of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD). Uroporphyrinogen III accumulates and is oxidized to photosensitive uroporphyrin, which deposits in skin and causes delayed blistering and skin fragility on sun-exposed areas. Alcohol use, iron overload, and hepatitis are well-known triggers, consistent with his elevated liver enzymes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PBGD deficiency causes acute intermittent porphyria with neurovisceral attacks, not delayed cutaneous blistering.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ferrochelatase deficiency causes erythropoietic protoporphyria, which produces painful burning photosensitivity that usually lacks blistering and presents in childhood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ALA synthase is the rate-limiting regulatory enzyme; PCT is notably not exacerbated by upregulation of this pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Uroporphyrinogen III synthase acts one step earlier and is not the enzyme implicated in PCT.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy develops intense burning, itching, and swelling of the skin within minutes of sun exposure, but rarely forms blisters. Over years, the backs of his hands have developed a waxy, pebbled thickening. Laboratory testing shows elevated protoporphyrin in red blood cells and dark-colored stool. Which enzyme is partially deficient?

explanation.summary: Erythropoietic protoporphyria results from partial ferrochelatase deficiency, causing painful photosensitivity with elevated RBC protoporphyrin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is caused by partial deficiency of ferrochelatase, the enzyme that inserts iron into protoporphyrin IX to form heme. Excess protoporphyrin accumulates in red blood cells, skin, and liver, generating reactive oxygen species on light exposure that cause painful, non-blistering photosensitivity beginning in childhood. Because protoporphyrin is water-insoluble, it is excreted in stool, giving it a dark color, and diagnosis relies on elevated RBC protoporphyrin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: UROD deficiency causes PCT, which produces frank blistering and skin fragility rather than the burning, non-blistering pain of EPP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PBGD deficiency causes acute intermittent porphyria with neurovisceral attacks, not childhood cutaneous photosensitivity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ALA dehydrase deficiency is an early cytosolic-step defect and would not cause protoporphyrin to accumulate in red cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coproporphyrinogen oxidase acts earlier in the pathway and does not produce the isolated protoporphyrin accumulation seen in EPP.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A researcher studies how dietary non-heme iron crosses the apical membrane of duodenal enterocytes. Non-heme iron in food is largely ferric (Fe3+) and cannot be taken up in that form. Which of the following correctly pairs the step with the protein responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fe3+ is oxidized to Fe2+ by hephaestin, then enters via ferroportin-1

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fe3+ binds transferrin at the lumen and enters via the transferrin receptor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fe3+ enters directly via ferroportin-1 without a change in oxidation state

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fe3+ is stored as ferritin at the apical surface before absorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by duodenal cytochrome B, then Fe2+ enters the enterocyte via DMT-1

explanation.summary: Dietary Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by duodenal cytochrome B and imported by DMT-1.
explanation.whyCorrect: Only ferrous iron (Fe2+) can be absorbed. Non-heme dietary Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by the vitamin C-dependent duodenal cytochrome B on the apical surface, and this ferrous iron is then transported into the enterocyte by the divalent metal transporter-1 (DMT-1).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hephaestin oxidizes Fe2+ to Fe3+ on the basal side after export; it does not act at apical uptake, and ferroportin exports rather than imports iron.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transferrin transports iron in the blood as Fe3+; it is not the mechanism for luminal uptake across the apical enterocyte membrane.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ferroportin-1 exports iron from the basolateral membrane into portal blood, not import at the apical surface, and iron must first be reduced.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ferritin is an intracellular storage protein, not an apical uptake mechanism for luminal iron.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 9-month-old infant has microcytic anemia unresponsive to oral iron. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in SLC11A2, the gene encoding DMT-1. Which of the following best explains the anemia in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Iron is trapped in endosomal vesicles and cannot be released

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hepcidin is overexpressed, degrading all ferroportin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transferrin cannot be synthesized by the liver

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ferrochelatase cannot insert iron into protoporphyrin IX

choices.label: E
choices.text: Excess iron is deposited as hemosiderin in macrophages

explanation.summary: A DMT-1 (SLC11A2) mutation traps iron intracellularly, causing functional iron deficiency and microcytic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: DMT-1 normally moves ferrous iron into cells and out of endosomes. When SLC11A2 is mutated, iron becomes trapped in endosomal vesicles and cannot be released to bind transferrin or be used in biosynthetic reactions. The resulting reduction in heme and globin synthesis produces a microcytic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The defect is in the iron transporter DMT-1, not in hepcidin; hepcidin overexpression is not the mechanism described in this inherited mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transferrin is synthesized normally by the liver; the problem is that iron cannot reach transferrin because it is trapped in vesicles.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ferrochelatase deficiency causes EPP, a porphyria, not a microcytic anemia from an SLC11A2 mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemosiderin deposition reflects iron overload, the opposite of the functional iron deficiency caused by DMT-1 loss.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)
