questions.stem: After ferroportin exports iron across the basolateral membrane of an enterocyte, the iron must be loaded onto plasma transferrin for transport. Because transferrin binds only ferric iron, which protein performs the required conversion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Duodenal cytochrome B, a vitamin C-dependent reductase

explanation.summary: Hephaestin, a copper-dependent ferroxidase, oxidizes exported Fe2+ to Fe3+ so it can bind transferrin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ferroportin exports iron in the ferrous (Fe2+) state, but transferrin transports only ferric (Fe3+) iron. Hephaestin is a copper-dependent ferroxidase that oxidizes the exported Fe2+ to Fe3+, enabling it to bind plasma transferrin for transport.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Duodenal cytochrome B reduces Fe3+ to Fe2+ at the apical surface for uptake; it performs the opposite reaction from what is needed here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepcidin regulates iron export by degrading ferroportin; it does not change the oxidation state of iron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DMT-1 transports ferrous iron across membranes but has no ferroxidase activity to load transferrin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ferritin stores iron intracellularly and does not oxidize iron for transferrin loading.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A hematology team reviews cases of hereditary hemochromatosis to identify the common consequence of an HFE gene mutation. Which of the following best describes the downstream effect of HFE dysfunction that produces iron overload?

explanation.summary: HFE mutations lower hepcidin activity, prolonging ferroportin function and driving iron overload.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hereditary hemochromatosis, an HFE mutation renders hepatocytes unable to produce adequately functioning hepcidin. Because hepcidin normally degrades ferroportin, reduced hepcidin allows ferroportin to remain active, prolonging iron export and increasing dietary iron absorption and release from macrophages. Homozygotes can absorb two to four times more iron than normal.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Increased hepcidin would degrade ferroportin and cause iron deficiency, the opposite of hemochromatosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Iron overload lowers TIBC because most transferrin is already saturated with iron, so TIBC does not increase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum iron is elevated in iron overload, not decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron absorption is increased in hemochromatosis, not decreased, because ferroportin export is prolonged.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man reports fatigue, joint pain in his hands, and progressively bronzed skin. He was recently diagnosed with diabetes mellitus, and exam reveals hepatomegaly. Iron studies show markedly elevated ferritin and transferrin saturation. Which of the following best accounts for the combination of skin pigmentation and diabetes seen in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Uroporphyrin deposition generating reactive oxygen species in the skin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accumulation of ALA and PBG damaging peripheral nerves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iron overload causing hyperpigmentation, insulin resistance, and suppressed insulin release

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protoporphyrin accumulation in red blood cells and bile

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hepcidin excess trapping iron within macrophages

explanation.summary: Hemochromatosis causes 'bronze diabetes' from iron-driven hyperpigmentation plus pancreatic and hepatic dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess iron accumulates in and damages the liver, heart, skin, and pancreas. In the skin it drives hyperpigmentation (increased melanocyte-stimulating hormone effect plus hemosiderin deposition), the liver develops cirrhosis leading to insulin resistance, and the pancreas shows decreased insulin production. The combined effect is the classic 'bronze diabetes' of hemochromatosis, supported here by high ferritin and transferrin saturation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Uroporphyrin-mediated skin damage occurs in porphyria cutanea tarda, which does not cause diabetes or the elevated iron studies described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALA and PBG neurotoxicity causes the acute neurovisceral attacks of AIP, not bronze skin with diabetes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Protoporphyrin accumulation characterizes EPP, which produces photosensitivity and gallstones, not diabetes and hyperpigmentation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hepcidin excess causes iron sequestration and anemia of chronic disease, the opposite of the iron-overload picture here.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman is found to have previously undiagnosed hereditary hemochromatosis. Compared with an unaffected person, which of the following patterns of iron studies would you most expect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serum iron increased, serum ferritin decreased, TIBC increased, transferrin saturation decreased

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serum iron decreased, serum ferritin increased, TIBC increased, transferrin saturation increased

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serum iron decreased, serum ferritin decreased, TIBC increased, transferrin saturation decreased

choices.label: D
choices.text: Serum iron increased, serum ferritin increased, TIBC decreased, transferrin saturation increased

choices.label: E
choices.text: Serum iron increased, serum ferritin increased, TIBC increased, transferrin saturation decreased

explanation.summary: Iron overload raises serum iron, ferritin, and transferrin saturation while lowering TIBC.
explanation.whyCorrect: In iron overload, excess circulating iron raises serum iron, and excess tissue stores spill ferritin into the serum. The abundance of iron saturates transferrin, raising transferrin saturation, while the amount of available unbound transferrin (TIBC) falls. Thus serum iron up, ferritin up, TIBC down, and transferrin saturation up.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This mixes overload and deficiency findings; ferritin and transferrin saturation should be increased, and TIBC decreased, in overload.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased serum iron and increased TIBC are features of iron deficiency, not overload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This is the classic iron-deficiency pattern, the opposite of hemochromatosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TIBC decreases and transferrin saturation increases in overload; both directions here are wrong.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis has a normocytic anemia. Labs show low serum iron, low TIBC, and an elevated serum ferritin. Which of the following best explains this pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic blood loss depleting total body iron stores

choices.label: B
choices.text: IL-6-driven inflammation raises hepcidin, reducing iron absorption

choices.label: C
choices.text: An HFE mutation lowering hepcidin activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deficiency of duodenal cytochrome B impairing dietary iron reduction

explanation.summary: Anemia of chronic disease reflects IL-6-induced hepcidin sequestering iron, giving low serum iron but high ferritin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inflammation, classically via the IL-6 cytokine, induces hepcidin. Elevated hepcidin degrades ferroportin, reducing iron absorption in the gut and iron release from macrophage stores. This protective sequestration lowers circulating serum iron and TIBC while ferritin (reflecting retained tissue stores) is normal or elevated, producing anemia of chronic disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chronic blood loss causes true iron deficiency with low ferritin and high TIBC, not the high ferritin and low TIBC seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An HFE mutation lowers hepcidin and causes iron overload, which would raise serum iron and transferrin saturation, not lower serum iron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Duodenal cytochrome B deficiency would impair absorption but does not explain the elevated ferritin of an inflammatory state.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ferrochelatase deficiency causes erythropoietic protoporphyria, a photosensitivity disorder, not this inflammatory iron-sequestration pattern.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A cell biologist examines how iron levels control ferritin production. Ferritin mRNA contains an iron response element (IRE) in its 5' untranslated region, which can be bound by iron regulatory protein (IRP). In a state of low intracellular iron, what happens to ferritin synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: IRP dissociates from the IRE, increasing ferritin synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: IRP binds the IRE and stabilizes the mRNA, increasing ferritin synthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ferritin is degraded into hemosiderin within lysosomes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hepcidin binds the IRE and enhances ferritin translation

choices.label: E
choices.text: IRP binds the IRE and blocks translation, decreasing ferritin synthesis

explanation.summary: In low iron, IRP binds the 5' IRE of ferritin mRNA and blocks its translation.
explanation.whyCorrect: IRP binds IRE-containing transcripts when intracellular iron is low. For ferritin mRNA, the IRE sits in the 5' untranslated region, so IRP binding physically blocks translation, decreasing ferritin synthesis. This is appropriate because a cell that is iron-poor should not waste resources building an iron storage protein.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IRP dissociates from the IRE when iron is high, not low; it is bound in the low-iron state.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IRP binding stabilizes mRNA for the transferrin receptor (3' IRE), but for ferritin (5' IRE) it blocks translation rather than stabilizing it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemosiderin forms from denatured ferritin in iron excess, which is unrelated to the low-iron translational control described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepcidin is a hormone that regulates ferroportin; it does not bind iron response elements or control ferritin translation.

questions.topicName: Porphyrins and Iron Homeostasis (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant is brought in for poor growth and a protuberant abdomen. He has doll-like facial features with fat cheeks. After a brief fast he develops a seizure; labs show severe hypoglycemia, elevated lactate, hypertriglyceridemia, and hyperuricemia. Exam reveals marked hepatomegaly. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes is the most likely cause?

explanation.summary: von Gierke disease (GSD type I) is glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency causing severe fasting hypoglycemia with lactic acidosis, hyperlipidemia, and hyperuricemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Without glucose-6-phosphatase the liver cannot convert glucose-6-phosphate to free exportable glucose, so both gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis are impaired. Accumulating glucose-6-phosphate spills into other pathways, producing lactic acidosis (impaired Cori cycle), hypertriglyceridemia, and hyperuricemia, along with hepatomegaly and doll-like facies.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency causes Pompe disease, which spares the liver's glucose output (normal glucose and pH) and presents with cardiomyopathy and hypotonia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency causes McArdle disease, an exercise-related muscle disorder without infantile hepatomegaly or fasting hypoglycemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Debranching enzyme deficiency causes Cori disease, which is milder with only mild hypoglycemia and characteristically no lactic acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Branching enzyme deficiency causes Andersen disease, marked by abnormal amylopectin-like glycogen and early cirrhosis rather than this metabolic picture.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 4-month-old boy has a large protruding tongue, profound hypotonia, and difficulty feeding. Exam reveals a markedly enlarged heart, and echocardiography demonstrates hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Fasting glucose and blood pH are normal, but serum creatine kinase is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Macroglossia, hypotonia, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with normal glucose and pH point to Pompe disease (lysosomal acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency).
explanation.whyCorrect: Pompe disease is a lysosomal storage disease in which glycogen accumulates in lysosomes and damages muscle fibers. The classic infantile form presents with macroglossia, weakness, hypotonia, and rapidly progressive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; because the liver functions normally there is no hypoglycemia or lactic acidosis, and creatine kinase is elevated from muscle damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: von Gierke disease affects the liver and would produce severe fasting hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis, not normal glucose and pH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cori disease causes hepatomegaly with mild hypoglycemia, not the hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and macroglossia seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: McArdle disease affects skeletal muscle and presents in adolescence or young adulthood with exercise intolerance, not in infancy with cardiomegaly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hers disease is a milder hepatic phosphorylase deficiency presenting with hypoglycemia and ketosis, not cardiomyopathy.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy reports muscle cramps and weakness within minutes of starting to run, but if he pushes through and slows down he can then continue comfortably for the rest of a race. After intense training his urine turns reddish-brown. Serum creatine kinase is elevated at rest. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains his presentation?

explanation.summary: Exercise-induced cramps with a second-wind phenomenon and myoglobinuria indicate McArdle disease (muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency).
explanation.whyCorrect: McArdle disease presents in adolescence with muscle cramps early in exercise and a pathognomonic second-wind phenomenon, in which increased blood flow delivers circulating glucose so activity can continue after the initial fatigue. Muscle breakdown produces rhabdomyolysis and myoglobinuria (dark urine) with elevated creatine kinase.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hepatic glycogen phosphorylase deficiency (Hers disease) affects the liver with hypoglycemia and ketosis, not exertional muscle symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency (Pompe) presents in infancy with cardiomyopathy and hypotonia, not adolescent exercise intolerance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency (von Gierke) causes hepatic hypoglycemia, not exercise-triggered myoglobinuria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscle phosphofructokinase deficiency (Tarui) causes similar exertional myopathy but characteristically LACKS the second-wind phenomenon this patient shows.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 2-year-old has mild fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and short stature. Unlike a related glycogen storage disease, her blood lactate is normal and she has no lactic acidosis. Liver biopsy shows glycogen with a normal branching pattern but abnormally short outer branches (limit dextrin). Which of the following best explains the absence of lactic acidosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gluconeogenesis remains intact, keeping the Cori cycle functional

choices.label: B
choices.text: Glucose-6-phosphatase activity is preserved and exports glucose normally

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lysosomal glycogen degradation is selectively impaired

choices.label: E
choices.text: Skeletal muscle is entirely spared from the enzyme defect

explanation.summary: In Cori disease (debranching enzyme deficiency), gluconeogenesis is unaffected, so the Cori cycle clears lactate and no lactic acidosis develops.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cori disease results from deficiency of the debranching enzyme (alpha-1,6-glucosidase), producing limit dextrin accumulation. Because the defect does not impede gluconeogenesis, lactate can still be converted to glucose in the liver, so unlike von Gierke disease these patients do not develop lactic acidosis and have only mild fasting hypoglycemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glucose-6-phosphatase is normal in Cori disease, but that is not the reason lactic acidosis is absent; the key is that gluconeogenesis and the Cori cycle stay intact.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Absent glycogen synthesis describes GSD type 0 (glycogen synthase deficiency), a different disorder that actually causes fasting lactic acidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Impaired lysosomal glycogen degradation describes Pompe disease, which is not the mechanism here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Muscle is not spared in Cori disease; debranching enzyme deficiency can cause muscle weakness and cardiomyopathy, and this does not explain the lactate finding.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy develops fatigue, scleral icterus, and dark urine two days after starting an antibiotic; he had also recently eaten fava beans. A peripheral blood smear shows Heinz bodies and bite cells. The underlying enzyme deficiency most directly leads to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inadequate NADPH, leading to oxidative injury of hemoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inability to reduce ferric iron within hemoglobin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Accumulation of limit dextrin within erythrocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced synthesis of the glutathione reductase protein itself

explanation.summary: G6PD deficiency reduces NADPH production, leaving red cells unable to neutralize reactive oxygen species, which oxidizes and damages hemoglobin.
explanation.whyCorrect: G6PD generates NADPH in the pentose phosphate pathway. Without adequate NADPH, glutathione cannot be kept reduced, so reactive oxygen species accumulate and oxidize hemoglobin. This produces Heinz bodies (oxidized Hb polymers) and bite cells, culminating in episodic hemolysis triggered by oxidative stressors such as drugs, infection, or fava beans.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Impaired glycolytic ATP production describes pyruvate kinase deficiency, which causes chronic hemolysis without an oxidative trigger, not Heinz bodies and bite cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inability to reduce ferric iron describes methemoglobinemia (cytochrome b5 reductase deficiency), which causes cyanosis rather than oxidative hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Limit dextrin accumulation is a feature of Cori disease in liver and muscle, not a red-cell process.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glutathione reductase function is lost because of missing NADPH cofactor, not because the reductase protein itself is deficient or under-synthesized.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A newborn is found to have chronic hemolytic anemia present since birth with no identifiable oxidative trigger. The peripheral smear shows spiculated (echinocyte) red cells, and 2,3-BPG is elevated. Which of the following best characterizes this disorder compared with G6PD deficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemolysis is chronic rather than episodic, and 2,3-BPG is increased

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hemolysis requires exposure to oxidant drugs or fava beans

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heinz bodies and bite cells are the characteristic smear finding

choices.label: D
choices.text: NADPH production through the pentose phosphate pathway is deficient

choices.label: E
choices.text: Red cells rely on mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation for ATP

explanation.summary: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolysis from birth (no trigger needed) and elevated 2,3-BPG, distinguishing it from the episodic, trigger-dependent hemolysis of G6PD deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because mature red cells rely solely on glycolysis for ATP, pyruvate kinase deficiency impairs ATP generation, producing rigid spiculated cells that undergo chronic hemolysis diagnosed early in life. The upstream glycolytic block redistributes intermediates through the shunt that generates 2,3-BPG, so 2,3-BPG is elevated.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Requiring an oxidant trigger describes G6PD deficiency; pyruvate kinase deficiency causes hemolysis without such triggers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heinz bodies and bite cells are hallmarks of oxidative hemolysis in G6PD deficiency, not pyruvate kinase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Deficient NADPH from the pentose phosphate pathway is the defect in G6PD deficiency, not pyruvate kinase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mature red cells lack mitochondria and depend on glycolysis for ATP, so they cannot use oxidative phosphorylation.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A cyanotic infant has an oxygen saturation that does not improve with supplemental oxygen, and arterial blood is described as chocolate-brown. Cyanosis resolves promptly after intravenous methylene blue. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes is the most likely underlying cause?

explanation.summary: Congenital methemoglobinemia results from cytochrome b5 reductase deficiency, leaving hemoglobin iron in the ferric (Fe3+) state.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cytochrome b5 reductase normally reconverts methemoglobin (ferric, Fe3+) back to functional hemoglobin (ferrous, Fe2+). When it is deficient, methemoglobin accumulates and cannot bind oxygen, producing cyanosis and chocolate-brown blood that does not respond to oxygen. Methylene blue reduces met-Hb to Hb in an NADPH-dependent manner, reversing the cyanosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolytic anemia, not methemoglobinemia or chocolate-brown blood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: G6PD deficiency causes episodic oxidative hemolysis; although severe oxidative stress can transiently form met-Hb, it is not the classic congenital cause described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency causes Pompe disease with cardiomyopathy, unrelated to cyanosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PEPCK deficiency impairs gluconeogenesis and causes fasting hypoglycemia, not methemoglobinemia.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A neonate has failure to thrive, severe lactic acidosis, hypotonia, and ataxia, with abnormalities on neuroimaging. A high-fat, low-carbohydrate (ketogenic) diet along with thiamine supplementation is initiated. Which of the following best explains why the ketogenic diet is beneficial in this disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ketone-derived acetyl-CoA bypasses the deficient pyruvate dehydrogenase step

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fatty acids stimulate residual glucose-6-phosphatase activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ketone bodies inhibit lactate production by pyruvate carboxylase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dietary fat provides substrate for hepatic glycogen synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ketosis restores debranching enzyme function in muscle

explanation.summary: In pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) complex deficiency, a ketogenic diet supplies acetyl-CoA from ketone bodies, bypassing the blocked pyruvate-to-acetyl-CoA step.
explanation.whyCorrect: PDH deficiency prevents pyruvate from being converted to acetyl-CoA, so glycolysis cannot be linked to the citric acid cycle and lactate builds up, severely affecting the brain. A ketogenic diet generates acetyl-CoA from ketone bodies instead of pyruvate, providing CNS fuel; vitamin cofactors like thiamine are tried to recover any residual enzyme activity.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The defect is in pyruvate dehydrogenase, not glucose-6-phosphatase, and fatty acids do not stimulate that enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The benefit comes from generating acetyl-CoA that bypasses PDH, not from inhibiting pyruvate carboxylase.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Providing substrate for glycogen synthesis would not address the block linking glycolysis to the TCA cycle.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Debranching enzyme is unrelated to PDH deficiency, and ketosis does not restore its function.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 3-month-old presents with fasting hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, hepatomegaly, and hypotonia. In addition, serum ammonia is markedly elevated. This hyperammonemia distinguishes the disorder from PEPCK deficiency and is best explained by a deficiency of which of the following enzymes?

explanation.summary: Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency causes fasting hypoglycemia and acidosis like PEPCK deficiency but is uniquely associated with hyperammonemia due to reduced oxaloacetate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate, an anaplerotic reaction that supports gluconeogenesis, the TCA cycle, and the urea cycle. When it is deficient, dampened oxaloacetate formation deprives the urea cycle of needed intermediates, producing hyperammonemia in addition to the fasting hypoglycemia and acidosis shared with PEPCK deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: PEPCK deficiency impairs gluconeogenesis and causes fasting hypoglycemia, but it does not produce hyperammonemia, which is the distinguishing feature here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PDH complex deficiency causes lactic acidosis with prominent neurologic disease, not the hyperammonemia that specifically defines this case.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency (von Gierke) causes hepatomegaly and hyperuricemia but is not characterized by hyperammonemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hepatic glycogen phosphorylase deficiency (Hers) causes mild hypoglycemia and ketosis, not hyperammonemia.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A child with confirmed glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency is noted to have persistently elevated serum uric acid and later develops gout, for which allopurinol is started. Which of the following mechanisms best accounts for the hyperuricemia in this disorder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess glucose-6-phosphate is shunted into the pentose phosphate pathway to overproduce purines, while lactate competes with urate for renal excretion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impaired purine salvage increases renal reabsorption of hypoxanthine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic hemolysis releases purines that raise uric acid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ketone bodies directly stimulate xanthine oxidase activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deficient renal gluconeogenesis increases tubular secretion of urate

explanation.summary: In von Gierke disease, hyperuricemia arises from purine overproduction via the pentose phosphate pathway plus reduced renal urate clearance because lactate competes for the same transporter.
explanation.whyCorrect: Accumulated glucose-6-phosphate is diverted into the pentose phosphate pathway, driving overproduction of purines and thus uric acid. At the same time, elevated lactate competes with urate for the shared renal transporter, impairing uric acid clearance. Both mechanisms raise serum uric acid, and allopurinol is used to manage resulting gout.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The problem is purine overproduction and impaired excretion, not enhanced renal reabsorption of hypoxanthine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: von Gierke disease is not a hemolytic disorder, so hemolysis-derived purines do not explain the hyperuricemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ketone bodies do not directly stimulate xanthine oxidase; the mechanism involves purine flux and lactate competition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Renal handling is impaired (reduced clearance), and there is no increase in tubular urate secretion in this disease.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 15-month-old has failure to thrive and progressive hepatomegaly with early cirrhosis. Liver biopsy shows abnormal, poorly branched glycogen resembling amylopectin, accompanied by an inflammatory reaction thought to reflect an immune response against the abnormal glycogen. The child dies before age 2. Deficiency of which of the following enzymes is most likely?

explanation.summary: Andersen disease (GSD type IV) is branching enzyme deficiency, producing amylopectin-like glycogen, early cirrhosis, and death by about 2 years.
explanation.whyCorrect: The branching enzyme forms alpha-1,6 glycosidic branches. Its deficiency yields abnormal, long-chain, poorly branched glycogen resembling amylopectin. This abnormal glycogen triggers an inflammatory immune response leading to early liver cirrhosis and death by roughly 2 years of age.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Debranching enzyme deficiency causes Cori disease, which produces limit dextrin (short branches), not long amylopectin-like polymers, and is comparatively milder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency (von Gierke) causes severe hypoglycemia with normal glycogen structure, not abnormal branching.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepatic glycogen phosphorylase deficiency (Hers) is a milder disorder with normal glycogen structure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glycogen synthase deficiency (GSD type 0) results in essentially absent glycogen, not abnormally structured amylopectin-like glycogen.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 5-year-old with a hereditary hemolytic anemia is admitted during an active hemolytic crisis. Which of the following laboratory findings is expected as a shared consequence of both G6PD deficiency and pyruvate kinase deficiency during ongoing hemolysis?

explanation.summary: Active hemolysis from either enzyme deficiency consumes haptoglobin as it binds released free hemoglobin, so serum haptoglobin falls.
explanation.whyCorrect: Haptoglobin binds cell-free hemoglobin released during hemolysis, and the complex is cleared by macrophages. During a hemolytic crisis the surplus of free hemoglobin depletes haptoglobin, so levels are low regardless of whether the hemolysis is oxidative (G6PD) or from impaired glycolytic ATP (pyruvate kinase).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Haptoglobin decreases, not increases, because it is consumed binding free hemoglobin during hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemolysis produces hyperbilirubinemia and jaundice, so total bilirubin rises rather than falls.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum LDH is elevated in hemolysis because it is released from lysed red cells, not decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The reticulocyte count increases as the marrow compensates for red-cell loss, so it is not decreased.

questions.topicName: Inborn Errors of Carbohydrate Metabolism

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy is evaluated for tonsils that appear strikingly orange-yellow on examination with no evidence of infection. A fasting lipid panel shows total cholesterol 100 mg/dL, HDL 5 mg/dL, LDL 50 mg/dL, and triglycerides 250 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Enlarged orange-yellow tonsils with low HDL and low total cholesterol is the classic presentation of Tangier disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tangier disease results from a deficiency of the ABC1 transporter, which normally promotes cholesterol efflux from peripheral tissue onto HDL. The failure of efflux causes intracellular cholesterol accumulation, producing low HDL and low overall cholesterol, and the characteristic orange-yellow discoloration and enlargement of the tonsils.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Familial hypercholesterolemia causes very high LDL cholesterol, not the low cholesterol and low HDL seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Familial hyperchylomicronemia produces triglycerides often exceeding 1,000 mg/dL, far higher than this panel.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Familial hypertriglyceridemia shows high VLDL/triglycerides with low HDL but does not cause orange tonsils or low total cholesterol.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MCAD deficiency disrupts fatty acid oxidation and causes hypoketotic hypoglycemia, not orange tonsils or an abnormal lipid panel.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed Tangier disease has intracellular accumulation of cholesterol within peripheral tissues. A defect in which of the following is most directly responsible for this abnormality?

explanation.summary: Tangier disease is caused by a deficiency of the ABC1 transporter that mediates cholesterol efflux from peripheral tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: ABC1 is the transporter that promotes cholesterol release from extra-hepatic tissue so it can be picked up by HDL via LCAT activity. When ABC1 is absent, cholesterol cannot leave the cell and accumulates intracellularly, and the serum shows low HDL and low total cholesterol.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Defective LDL receptors cause familial hypercholesterolemia with high LDL, not intracellular cholesterol accumulation with low HDL.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lipoprotein lipase deficiency impairs triglyceride clearance and causes familial hyperchylomicronemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Apolipoprotein E defects cause familial dysbetalipoproteinemia with retained IDL and chylomicron remnants.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The OCTN2 transporter handles carnitine reabsorption and its defect causes primary carnitine deficiency, not Tangier disease.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with an increasing number of large, bumpy growths over her knees and buttocks as well as distinctive yellow-orange streaks in the creases of her palms. Both her mother and father developed heart disease before age 40. Her serum cholesterol and triglycerides are both elevated. Which of the following primary lipid disorders is most likely?

explanation.summary: Palmar xanthomas plus tuberoeruptive xanthomas with elevated cholesterol and triglycerides point to familial dysbetalipoproteinemia (type III).
explanation.whyCorrect: Palmar xanthomas along the palmar creases are pathognomonic for familial dysbetalipoproteinemia, which also commonly causes tuberoeruptive xanthomas on the knees and elbows. The disorder stems from an apolipoprotein E defect that impairs clearance of chylomicron and VLDL remnants, producing moderate elevations in both cholesterol and triglycerides and premature atherosclerotic heart disease, consistent with the family history.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Familial combined hyperlipidemia causes elevated cholesterol and triglycerides but does not produce pathognomonic palmar xanthomas.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Familial hypercholesterolemia raises cholesterol with near-normal triglycerides and does not cause palmar xanthomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Familial hyperchylomicronemia presents with markedly high triglycerides and near-normal cholesterol, without palmar xanthomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Familial hypertriglyceridemia shows high triglycerides with relatively normal cholesterol and lacks palmar xanthomas.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A newborn from a family with a known lipid disorder is found to have very high LDL cholesterol at birth with normal triglycerides. The child's affected relatives suffered myocardial infarctions in early adulthood. Which of the following best explains the underlying defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overproduction of VLDL by the liver

choices.label: C
choices.text: Too few or absent LDL receptors

explanation.summary: Familial hypercholesterolemia results from too few or absent LDL receptors, producing very high LDL with normal triglycerides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Familial hypercholesterolemia (type IIa) is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations that reduce or eliminate functional LDL receptors, most often in the LDL receptor gene itself. Without adequate receptors, LDL cannot be cleared, so LDL cholesterol is very high even at birth while triglycerides remain near normal, driving early atherosclerosis and myocardial infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: VLDL overproduction underlies familial hypertriglyceridemia, which elevates triglycerides rather than LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lipoprotein lipase deficiency causes familial hyperchylomicronemia with very high triglycerides, not isolated high LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A defective apolipoprotein E causes familial dysbetalipoproteinemia with elevated remnants and both cholesterol and triglycerides.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ABC1 transporter deficiency causes Tangier disease with low HDL and low total cholesterol.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 9-month-old infant is admitted with severe abdominal pain, and a fasting lipid panel reveals triglycerides of 1,400 mg/dL with cholesterol near the reference range. Genetic testing confirms a deficiency of lipoprotein lipase. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following complications?

explanation.summary: Familial hyperchylomicronemia causes markedly high triglycerides that place patients primarily at risk for pancreatitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lipoprotein lipase (or apo C-II) deficiency impairs degradation and clearance of triglyceride-rich chylomicrons, so triglycerides often exceed 1,000 mg/dL. These patients are primarily at risk for acute pancreatitis. Because cholesterol stays within or near reference ranges, the risk for ischemic heart disease is not increased.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The risk for ischemic heart disease is not increased in familial hyperchylomicronemia because cholesterol is near normal.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stroke is an atherosclerotic complication tied to elevated LDL, which is not the abnormality here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The brick does not attribute hepatic cirrhosis to this disorder; pancreatitis is the primary risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Peripheral arterial disease reflects atherosclerosis, which is not the main risk given near-normal cholesterol.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man has a lipid panel showing moderately elevated VLDL and triglycerides with a total cholesterol near the reference range and low HDL. He has no palmar xanthomas, and the disorder is attributed to hepatic overproduction of a single lipoprotein class. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Familial hypertriglyceridemia (type IV) is driven by hepatic overproduction of VLDL, elevating VLDL and triglycerides with near-normal cholesterol.
explanation.whyCorrect: Familial hypertriglyceridemia results from hepatic overproduction of VLDL, so the lipid panel shows elevated VLDL and triglycerides while total cholesterol stays relatively normal and HDL is often low. Because LDL is not increased, a strong association with atherosclerotic heart disease has not been found, though high triglycerides raise the risk for acute pancreatitis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Familial hypercholesterolemia produces very high LDL cholesterol with normal triglycerides, not the reverse.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia elevates IDL and chylomicron remnants and typically causes palmar xanthomas.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tangier disease causes low HDL and low total cholesterol from an ABC1 transporter defect, not VLDL overproduction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Familial hyperchylomicronemia elevates chylomicrons from LPL deficiency rather than VLDL overproduction by the liver.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a strong family history of early heart disease has a lipid panel showing elevated LDL, elevated VLDL, elevated triglycerides, and low HDL. Analysis reveals increased apolipoprotein B-100 with hepatic overproduction of small VLDL and dense LDL particles. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Combined elevation of LDL, VLDL, and triglycerides with increased apo B-100 is characteristic of familial combined hyperlipidemia (type IIb).
explanation.whyCorrect: Familial combined hyperlipidemia is the most common primary lipid disorder overall and is polygenic. Mutations that increase apolipoprotein B-100 drive hepatic overproduction of small VLDL and small dense LDL particles, and decreased hepatic LDL clearance from increased PCSK9 activity contributes as well. Patients show combined elevations of LDL, VLDL, and triglycerides with low HDL and accelerated atherosclerosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Familial hypercholesterolemia raises LDL alone with normal triglycerides, not a combined pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Familial hyperchylomicronemia elevates chylomicrons and triglycerides from LPL deficiency, without high LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Familial hypertriglyceridemia elevates VLDL and triglycerides but does not increase LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tangier disease shows low HDL and low total cholesterol rather than combined elevations.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A researcher is studying a monoclonal antibody that inhibits PCSK9 as a therapy for dyslipidemia. Inhibiting PCSK9 lowers circulating LDL by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blocking lipoprotein lipase on endothelial cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhancing cholesterol efflux through the ABC1 transporter

explanation.summary: PCSK9 normally targets LDL receptors for lysosomal degradation, so inhibiting it preserves receptors and lowers LDL.
explanation.whyCorrect: PCSK9 binds LDL receptors on hepatocytes; the complex is internalized and directed to lysosomes for degradation, preventing the receptor from recycling back to the cell surface. Inhibiting PCSK9 with a monoclonal antibody spares the receptors so they return to the surface and continue clearing LDL, lowering circulating LDL levels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increasing cholesterol synthesis would raise, not lower, circulating lipids and is not how PCSK9 inhibition works.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PCSK9 does not act on lipoprotein lipase; blocking LPL would impair triglyceride clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ABC1-mediated efflux is the pathway defective in Tangier disease and is unrelated to PCSK9's action on LDL receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stimulating VLDL overproduction would worsen dyslipidemia and is not the mechanism of PCSK9 inhibitors.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: In the early atherosclerotic lesion of a patient with familial hypercholesterolemia, macrophages within the arterial wall become engorged with lipid and transform into foam cells. Which of the following best describes the process that generates these foam cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Macrophages take up oxidized LDL through scavenger receptors

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrophages clear chylomicron remnants through apolipoprotein E receptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Macrophages accumulate carnitine because of an OCTN2 defect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Macrophages overproduce VLDL and store it internally

choices.label: E
choices.text: Macrophages fail to efflux cholesterol because of an ABC1 defect

explanation.summary: Foam cells form when macrophages take up oxidized LDL via low-affinity, nonregulated scavenger receptors.
explanation.whyCorrect: When LDL is abundant it becomes more susceptible to oxidative damage, generating the pro-atherogenic oxLDL particle. Monocytes adhere to injured endothelium and become macrophages, which take up modified (oxidized) LDL through nonspecific scavenger receptors that are not downregulated by intracellular cholesterol. This unchecked uptake fills the macrophage with lipid, transforming it into a foam cell.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Apolipoprotein E-mediated remnant clearance occurs in the liver and is not the mechanism of foam cell formation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carnitine accumulation from an OCTN2 defect relates to fatty acid oxidation, not foam cell formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Foam cells form by taking up oxidized LDL, not by internally overproducing VLDL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An ABC1 efflux defect describes Tangier disease; foam cells here form from oxLDL uptake via scavenger receptors.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A patient's lipid panel is described as demonstrating reverse cholesterol transport in which a lipoprotein carries cholesterol from peripheral tissues back to the liver and donates apolipoproteins to other particles. Blood levels of this lipoprotein are inversely related to cardiac disease. Which lipoprotein is being described?

explanation.summary: HDL performs reverse cholesterol transport and donates apolipoproteins, and its levels are inversely related to cardiac disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: HDL delivers cholesterol from the tissues back to the liver, a process known as reverse cholesterol transport. It also acts as a 'helper,' donating important apolipoproteins to other lipoproteins so they can be recognized and taken up by the appropriate tissues. HDL blood levels are inversely related to cardiac disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chylomicrons deliver dietary triglycerides and cholesterol from the intestines to peripheral tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.B: VLDL delivers liver-synthesized triglycerides to tissues, not cholesterol back to the liver.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IDL is a transitional particle between VLDL and LDL and does not carry out reverse cholesterol transport.
whyWrongByChoice.D: LDL delivers cholesterol from the liver to the tissues and its elevation is directly linked to cardiac disease.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 10-month-old infant develops lethargy and hypoglycemia during a viral illness with poor oral intake. Laboratory studies show low glucose with inappropriately low ketones. The child is subsequently diagnosed with medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD) deficiency. The hypoketotic hypoglycemia in this disorder is best explained by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess acetyl-CoA overwhelming the citric acid cycle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure to produce acetyl-CoA from beta-oxidation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased urinary loss of carnitine through OCTN2

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overproduction of ketone bodies by the liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Deficient LDL receptor recycling in hepatocytes

explanation.summary: Both MCAD and carnitine deficiency cause hypoketotic hypoglycemia because impaired beta-oxidation fails to generate acetyl-CoA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypoketotic hypoglycemia results from a failure to produce acetyl-CoA through beta-oxidation. Acetyl-CoA is the required substrate for ketogenesis, its generation supplies ATP needed for gluconeogenesis, and it is an allosteric activator of pyruvate carboxylase. When beta-oxidation is blocked, ketones cannot be made and glucose cannot be maintained, a state worsened by fasting or illness.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The problem is too little acetyl-CoA from blocked beta-oxidation, not an excess.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Urinary carnitine wasting is the mechanism in primary carnitine deficiency, not the direct cause of hypoketotic hypoglycemia in MCAD deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ketone production is decreased, not overproduced, which is why the hypoglycemia is hypoketotic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LDL receptor recycling relates to cholesterol handling and has no role in this fatty acid oxidation defect.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A dietitian is planning nutritional supplementation for two patients: one with systemic primary carnitine deficiency and one with MCAD deficiency. Which dietary supplement could be considered for the patient with carnitine deficiency but would not be appropriate for the patient with MCAD deficiency?

explanation.summary: Medium-chain fatty acids bypass carnitine transport and are useful in carnitine deficiency but cannot be metabolized in MCAD deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Medium-chain fatty acids freely diffuse into the cell and mitochondria, bypassing the need for carnitine, so they can be metabolized in primary carnitine deficiency. In MCAD deficiency, however, the enzyme needed to catabolize these very substrates is missing, so MCFAs would only accumulate. MCFA supplementation is therefore considered only for carnitine deficiency, not MCAD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Long-chain fatty acids should be avoided in both deficiencies because neither can properly catabolize them.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Short-chain fatty acids can be strategically provided in both deficiencies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carnitine supplementation is considered in both conditions and is routine in primary carnitine deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glucose supports both patients by preventing catabolic states and is not contraindicated in either.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism

questions.stem: A 6-month-old boy of Ashkenazi Jewish descent, who developed normally until now, is losing previously acquired motor skills and demonstrates an exaggerated startle response to loud sounds. Fundoscopic exam reveals a cherry-red spot on the macula. The liver and spleen are not enlarged. Which of the following enzyme deficiencies is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: Tay-Sachs disease is caused by hexosaminidase A deficiency with GM2 ganglioside accumulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The infantile cherry-red macula, exaggerated startle response reflecting abnormal brainstem function, progressive loss of motor skills, and absence of hepatosplenomegaly in an Ashkenazi Jewish infant are classic for Tay-Sachs disease. Hexosaminidase A deficiency leads to accumulation of GM2 ganglioside, which is critical for neuronal health.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Sphingomyelinase deficiency causes Niemann-Pick disease, which shares the cherry-red spot but characteristically presents with prominent hepatosplenomegaly, absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency causes Gaucher disease, marked by hepatosplenomegaly and bone disease rather than a cherry-red spot with a normal-sized liver and spleen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency causes Fabry disease, an adult-onset X-linked disorder with angiokeratomas and acroparesthesia, not infantile neurodegeneration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Galactocerebrosidase deficiency causes Krabbe disease, which features globoid cells and optic atrophy rather than a cherry-red macular spot with startle response.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 5-month-old infant who was healthy at birth now has failure to thrive, spasticity, and rapidly progressing neurologic deterioration. On exam there is massive hepatosplenomegaly, and fundoscopy shows a cherry-red spot on the macula. The infant dies at 30 months of age. Which enzyme deficiency best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Niemann-Pick disease type A results from sphingomyelinase deficiency and accumulation of sphingomyelin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of a cherry-red macular spot AND massive hepatosplenomegaly with rapidly progressive neurologic decline and death by about age 3 is characteristic of Niemann-Pick disease type A. It is caused by a deficiency of sphingomyelinase, leading to accumulation of sphingomyelin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hexosaminidase A deficiency (Tay-Sachs) also causes a cherry-red spot, but there is no hepatosplenomegaly, which is a prominent finding here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry) is an adult-onset X-linked disorder with angiokeratomas and renal/cardiac disease, not infantile hepatosplenomegaly with a cherry-red spot.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency (Gaucher type 1) causes hepatosplenomegaly and bone disease but not a cherry-red macular spot or this fulminant infantile neurodegeneration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Galactocerebrosidase deficiency (Krabbe) spares the liver and spleen and does not produce a cherry-red macular spot.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: Two infants each present with progressive neurodegeneration and a cherry-red spot on the macula. Which of the following findings would most reliably distinguish Niemann-Pick disease from Tay-Sachs disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of a cherry-red spot on the macula

explanation.summary: Hepatosplenomegaly is present in Niemann-Pick but absent in Tay-Sachs, distinguishing the two.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both diseases share a cherry-red macular spot and infantile progressive neurodegeneration, so those features cannot separate them. The brick specifically notes that the absence of hepatosplenomegaly helps distinguish Tay-Sachs from Niemann-Pick, in which hepatosplenomegaly is a prominent finding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A cherry-red spot occurs in both conditions and therefore cannot distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An increased startle response reflects brainstem dysfunction seen with progressive neurodegeneration and is not specific to one disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Both disorders present in infancy, so onset timing does not separate them.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Both are progressive, fatal neurodegenerative diseases, so this feature is shared.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with chronic bone pain, anemia, easy bruising, and an abdomen distended by a massively enlarged spleen. A bone marrow biopsy shows lipid-laden macrophages with a 'crinkled paper' or 'wrinkled silk' cytoplasmic appearance. Which enzyme deficiency is most consistent with these findings?

explanation.summary: Gaucher disease is caused by glucocerebrosidase deficiency and features Gaucher cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepatosplenomegaly with prominent splenomegaly, anemia, thrombocytopenia causing easy bruising, bone pain, and lipid-laden macrophages with a crinkled-paper appearance (Gaucher cells) define Gaucher disease, the most common lysosomal storage disease. It results from glucocerebrosidase deficiency and accumulation of glucocerebroside.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Sphingomyelinase deficiency (Niemann-Pick) causes hepatosplenomegaly but is associated with a cherry-red spot and, in type A, fatal infantile neurodegeneration rather than crinkled-paper macrophages.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry) causes angiokeratomas, acroparesthesia, and renal/cardiac disease, not Gaucher cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hexosaminidase A deficiency (Tay-Sachs) causes infantile neurodegeneration with a cherry-red spot and no hepatosplenomegaly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Galactocerebrosidase deficiency (Krabbe) produces globoid cells and neurologic disease, sparing the spleen and liver.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with Gaucher disease is started on glucocerebrosidase enzyme replacement therapy. Compared with the untreated natural history of other lysosomal storage diseases discussed, which statement about his expected outcome is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enzyme replacement therapy can allow him to live a relatively long, normal life

choices.label: B
choices.text: Death is expected within 2 to 3 years regardless of therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Therapy will reverse an existing cherry-red macular spot

choices.label: D
choices.text: He will inevitably develop severe central nervous system degeneration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Therapy prevents accumulation of very-long-chain fatty acids

explanation.summary: Type 1 Gaucher disease is treatable, and enzyme replacement therapy supports a relatively normal lifespan.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick emphasizes that Gaucher disease is treatable: with glucocerebrosidase enzyme replacement therapy, patients can live relatively long, normal lives. This is a notable contrast to most other lysosomal storage diseases, which are fatal at a young age.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Death within 2 to 3 years describes untreated Tay-Sachs disease, not treated Gaucher disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A cherry-red spot is a feature of Tay-Sachs and Niemann-Pick disease, not Gaucher disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type 1 (non-neuronopathic) Gaucher disease accounts for 99% of cases and does not involve the central nervous system.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Very-long-chain fatty acid accumulation is a feature of peroxisomal disorders, not Gaucher disease, and is unrelated to glucocerebrosidase therapy.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man reports years of burning pain and tingling in his hands and feet, markedly decreased sweating, and intermittent abdominal cramping. Examination reveals clusters of small, raised reddish-purple skin lesions, and urinalysis shows proteinuria. His maternal uncle died of renal failure at a young age. Which enzyme is most likely deficient?

explanation.summary: Fabry disease is an X-linked deficiency of alpha-galactosidase A with ceramide trihexoside accumulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acroparesthesia (burning/tingling of the hands and feet), hypohidrosis, angiokeratomas (raised reddish-purple skin lesions), and renal disease in a male with an X-linked family pattern are classic for Fabry disease. It results from deficiency of alpha-galactosidase A, causing accumulation of ceramide trihexoside.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency (Gaucher) causes splenomegaly, anemia, and bone disease, not angiokeratomas and acroparesthesia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hexosaminidase A deficiency (Tay-Sachs) is a fatal infantile neurodegenerative disorder, not an adult-onset disease with skin and peripheral nerve findings.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sphingomyelinase deficiency (Niemann-Pick) causes hepatosplenomegaly and a cherry-red spot, not this constellation of adult symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Galactocerebrosidase deficiency (Krabbe) is an infantile demyelinating disease, not an adult disorder with angiokeratomas.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with a long history of hypohidrosis, angiokeratomas, and acroparesthesia is found to have deficient alpha-galactosidase A activity. As his disease progresses, sphingolipid accumulates in the vascular endothelial lining. Which of the following complications is most characteristically associated with this process?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Globoid cell formation in nervous tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: Massive hepatosplenomegaly with a huge spleen

explanation.summary: In Fabry disease, sphingolipid deposition in vascular endothelium promotes early stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that Fabry disease produces early stroke because of sphingolipid accumulation in the vascular endothelial lining, along with renal and cardiac complications that can lead to death in the third or fourth decade.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A cherry-red macular spot is a feature of Tay-Sachs and Niemann-Pick disease, not Fabry disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Globoid cells are pathognomonic of Krabbe disease, not Fabry disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Massive splenomegaly is characteristic of Gaucher disease; Fabry disease does not cause a hugely enlarged spleen.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Crinkled-paper macrophages are Gaucher cells, seen in Gaucher disease rather than Fabry disease.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A 5-month-old infant who was normal at birth now has failure to thrive, marked irritability, and progressive vision loss due to optic atrophy, along with seizure-like episodes that do not respond to anticonvulsant therapy. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Nervous tissue shows multinucleated macrophages with abundant cytoplasm. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely?

explanation.summary: Krabbe disease results from galactocerebrosidase deficiency and produces globoid cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Infantile onset at 4-6 months with failure to thrive, irritability, optic atrophy with vision loss, seizures unresponsive to anticonvulsants, absence of liver and spleen involvement, and multinucleated globoid cells are hallmarks of Krabbe disease. It is caused by galactocerebrosidase deficiency with accumulation of galactocerebroside, a major component of myelin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hexosaminidase A deficiency (Tay-Sachs) causes a cherry-red spot and startle response, not globoid cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sphingomyelinase deficiency (Niemann-Pick) causes hepatosplenomegaly and a cherry-red spot, both absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency (Gaucher) causes splenomegaly and bone disease with Gaucher cells, not globoid cells or optic atrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alpha-galactosidase A deficiency (Fabry) is an adult-onset disorder with angiokeratomas, not an infantile demyelinating disease.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A neonate presents with severe hypotonia, seizures, jaundice, and hepatomegaly. Physical exam shows a high forehead, widely spaced eyes, and a broad nasal bridge. Laboratory testing reveals elevated very-long-chain fatty acids, and tissue analysis shows that peroxisomes are essentially absent. This disorder is inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion. Which underlying defect is most likely responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defective PEX proteins impairing peroxisome biogenesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: A defective ABC transporter for fatty acid import

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deficiency of a mitochondrial beta-oxidation enzyme

explanation.summary: Zellweger syndrome is caused by defective PEX proteins, leading to failed peroxisome biogenesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Zellweger syndrome is an autosomal recessive disorder of the PEX gene causing faulty peroxisomal biogenesis. Without functional peroxins, peroxisomal enzymes cannot be imported, so peroxisomes are absent or nonfunctional, VLCFAs accumulate in liver and brain, and infants show craniofacial anomalies, hypotonia, seizures, jaundice, and hepatomegaly, usually dying before age 1.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A deficient lysosomal hydrolase would cause a lysosomal storage disease with substrate accumulation, not an absence of peroxisomes with elevated VLCFAs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A defective ABC transporter underlies X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy, in which peroxisomes still form; here peroxisomes are essentially absent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A mitochondrial beta-oxidation defect would not eliminate peroxisomes or produce this dysmorphic biogenesis phenotype.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sphingomyelinase deficiency causes Niemann-Pick disease, a lysosomal disorder unrelated to peroxisome biogenesis.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy develops progressive neurologic decline together with signs of adrenal insufficiency, including fatigue and skin hyperpigmentation. Plasma very-long-chain fatty acids are elevated, but peroxisomes are present in his tissues. The condition is inherited in an X-linked pattern. Which molecular defect best explains this presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A defective ABC transporter for very-long-chain fatty acids

explanation.summary: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy is caused by a defective ABC transporter that imports VLCFA substrates into peroxisomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy occurs when transport of VLCFA substrates into the peroxisome is defective due to a faulty ABC transporter that normally uses ATP hydrolysis. VLCFAs accumulate in blood and tissues, and the disease presents later in childhood with neurologic decline and adrenal insufficiency, with peroxisomes still present.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Absent peroxins causing failed peroxisome formation describes Zellweger syndrome, in which peroxisomes are absent; here they are present.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency causes Gaucher disease, a lysosomal disorder with hepatosplenomegaly and bone disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hexosaminidase A deficiency causes Tay-Sachs disease, an infantile neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Catalase reduces hydrogen peroxide to water; its deficiency does not explain elevated VLCFAs with adrenal and neurologic disease.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A researcher studies isolated peroxisomes and notes that their beta-oxidation reactions transfer electrons directly to oxygen, generating hydrogen peroxide rather than driving ATP synthesis. Which peroxisomal enzyme is chiefly responsible for protecting the cell from the toxic hydrogen peroxide produced?

explanation.summary: Catalase in peroxisomes reduces toxic hydrogen peroxide to water.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because hydrogen peroxide is toxic, it is sequestered within the peroxisome, which contains catalase to reduce hydrogen peroxide to water and protect the cell from damage.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acyl-CoA oxidase catalyzes the first step of peroxisomal beta-oxidation and actually generates hydrogen peroxide rather than detoxifying it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carnitine acetyltransferase transports activated acyl groups out of the peroxisome toward the mitochondria; it does not neutralize hydrogen peroxide.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucocerebrosidase is a lysosomal enzyme deficient in Gaucher disease, not a peroxisomal antioxidant.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sphingomyelinase is a lysosomal enzyme deficient in Niemann-Pick disease, unrelated to peroxisomal hydrogen peroxide handling.

questions.topicName: Dyslipidemias, Lipid Storage Disorders, and other defects in Lipid Metabolism (2 of 2)

questions.stem: A biochemistry student is asked to describe how peroxisomal beta-oxidation differs from mitochondrial beta-oxidation. Which of the following best characterizes the substrate handled specifically by peroxisomal beta-oxidation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Very-long-chain fatty acids greater than 22 carbons

choices.label: B
choices.text: Short-chain fatty acids that are fully oxidized to carbon dioxide within the peroxisome

choices.label: C
choices.text: Glycogen, which is degraded to glucose within the peroxisome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Glycosaminoglycans that are broken down into monosaccharides

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sphingolipids requiring a unique acid hydrolase active at pH 5

explanation.summary: Peroxisomal beta-oxidation shortens very-long-chain fatty acids too large for mitochondria into medium-chain products.
explanation.whyCorrect: Peroxisomal beta-oxidation breaks down very-long-chain fatty acids larger than 22 carbons, which are too big for transport into mitochondria. It produces medium-chain fatty acids that are then transported to mitochondria to complete beta-oxidation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Peroxisomal beta-oxidation does not fully oxidize substrates to carbon dioxide; it shortens VLCFAs and hands medium-chain products to mitochondria.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glycogen degradation defects (Pompe disease) involve lysosomes, not peroxisomal beta-oxidation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glycosaminoglycan (mucopolysaccharide) degradation is impaired in Hunter and Hurler syndromes, which are lysosomal, not peroxisomal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Enzymes active at pH 5 are lysosomal acid hydrolases used for sphingolipid turnover, not peroxisomal beta-oxidation of fatty acids.
